new_findings.title: Warm fans with a slightly discounted first PPV, then escalate price on every subsequent send

variants.claim: The first PPV sale is a warm-up purchase at a slight discount; once a fan buys it, every future PPV should be priced at the floor or higher, escalating as the conversation progresses.
variants.what_they_do: New subscribers receive an automated script that pitches a first video at $34.99 (slightly below the $39.99 floor) to reduce friction and get a first purchase. After that purchase the fan is considered 'warmed up' and all future content is priced at $40+ for video and $20+ for images, escalating upward as the sexting session progresses. The escalation is tied to emotional momentum — the longer the conversation runs, the more the fan will pay.

variants.claim: Pricing the first PPV low (roughly $5-$20) exists to get the fan used to spending, not to make money on that sale.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard caps the first PPV at $5-$20, never higher, to get the customer used to the habit of buying on the platform before laddering up; habibi tests a brand-new buyer with a small first buy (e.g. $9) to read his boundaries.

variants.claim: The first PPV sale should always be $5 or less to condition the subscriber to buy; subsequent sales increase in price incrementally, and the overall set is structured as 7 separate sales attempts at progressively higher price points.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are instructed to structure one content 'set' as 7 distinct sales opportunities (individual pics, pic+video combos, etc.) with prices rising at each step. The first sale is always ≤$5 — not because the content is cheap, but to make the buy frictionless and establish a Pavlovian habit: sub sees PPV → sub unlocks. Subs who won't spend $5 are deprioritized or ignored. New chatters are taught to use small price increments (e.g., $5 → $7 → $10) rather than jumping to $25+. Selling too much too soon is explicitly called out as a sub-burning mistake; longevity and lifetime value are the stated goal.

variants.claim: Starting the PPV ladder at a low price ($15) minimizes the psychological barrier to the first purchase, making it far more likely the subscriber enters the escalation funnel and ultimately spends $300–$500.
variants.what_they_do: The specific ladder cited: $15 → $30 → $60 → $120 → $200 → $200. Entry price is low enough to feel trivial. Each step roughly doubles, creating anchoring where each subsequent price feels proportional to the last rather than shocking. The final $200 items are 'full action' — maximum explicitness justifying maximum price. Total session value: ~$625 at full conversion.

variants.claim: Sending low-ticket PPVs ($15–$20) first gauges a subscriber's willingness to spend and builds purchasing habit before high-ticket ($80–$100+) asks.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter begins by offering content in the $15–$20 range ('dipping a toe in the water'). After multiple purchases at this level, the chatter reads the subscriber's comfort and escalates to high-ticket PPVs ($80, $90, $100+). No high-ticket offer is sent cold to a new subscriber.

variants.claim: Setting price per fan from their purchase history, backing off after a no and pushing higher for proven whales, beats one fixed price for all.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard uses dynamic pricing read from each subscriber's unlock history via tools like Infloww: if a fan stalled at $65 he offers 17/27/47/59 next time, but if a fan will pay $150-$200 for what others pay $20 for he sells at that willingness, and he keeps explicit content premium-priced so perceived value stays high.

sources.quote: it will sell them a video for $34.99 yes it is slightly cheaper than the $40 I mentioned earlier but this is to warm them up
once they purchase this $34.99 video then you can consider them warmed up and then whenever you sell them a piece of content after that it is going to be at least 40 and upwards
price the very first pbv between $5 to $20 let's say not go any higher than that... get them used to spending
the last one nine bucks this was a first buy for him... I wanted to test his boundaries
the first sale should be under $5 should be $5 or less why because everyone can afford $5
seven separate sales so it could be one you know number one could be one pick number two could be a pick in a video
you have the first video for like $15 second video for 30 then for 60 for 120 for 200 the next one for 200
if he buys an entire script that's about $300 to $500 you make per subscriber

you'll start off selling subscriber content from $15 to $20 so it's kind of like dipping into the water seeing where they're at and then after the subscribers purchase multiple pieces you can start selling PPVs for high ticket prices like 80 bucks 90 100
you pick a different selling strategy for every single subscriber
the low price might make him not want to buy instead of buy... keep the content premium priced

palm_comparison.vs_us: No Palm-side pricing ladder or escalation logic exists (§7 GAP). The external chat team sets its own approach; Palm has no documented position on warm-up pricing or escalation sequencing.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a two-phase pricing ladder for the external chat team and future in-house chatters: Phase 1 (new sub warm-up) = first video at ~$35; Phase 2 (warmed fan) = $40+ escalating. Write this into the chat team brief and eventually into Palm's in-house chatter SOP (§8 build-toward).

new_findings.topic: signing call vibe and red flags
new_findings.title: Treat the signing call as a two-way interview with hard non-negotiables

variants.claim: Operators sign more durable creators by qualifying them on the call against work-ethic and commitment non-negotiables instead of signing whoever replies.
variants.what_they_do: Opens the call human-first (camera on, 'how are you', hobbies) to break barriers, then qualifies: rejects short-term-money motives and 'overly involved' creators, requires committed daily hours and a fixed content/live cadence, and walks away if the vibe or non-negotiables fail.

variants.claim: Sign only creators who fit a defined ideal-client profile and screen out predictable red-flag situations rather than chasing anyone with a link in bio.
variants.what_they_do: Ideal client: already on OnlyFans, has/open to a niche, posts consistently, communicates, pays on time, has some IG/Twitter/TikTok/Reddit presence, and is coachable. Qualify briefly in DMs then on a call. Red flags that get declined or probed: under 3-4 months on OF, about to travel/move/have a baby, just had a breakup, churned through 5+ agencies recently, know-it-all who rejects all advice, asks zero questions, takes weeks to sign.

variants.claim: Agencies should qualify creators on mindset — specifically filtering out those who want purely passive income — because they disengage during the experimentation phase and waste agency resources.
variants.what_they_do: The speakers describe two acquisition filters: (1) prefer creators who have already tried OF themselves and seen some traction, because they understand the work involved and appreciate agency value more; (2) screen out creators who articulate 'I just want to make money passively' as their primary motivation, since they won't fulfill content obligations and the agency's marketing investment becomes a gamble. They also describe a 7-step creator vetting process for creators evaluating agencies (social proof → documented process → call → ask deliverable questions → watch for red flags like guaranteed numbers or pressure to sign quickly), which implicitly describes what a credible agency's pitch should contain.

sources.quote: work ethic trumps everything
have non-negotiables and have some respect for yourself
we prefer it's between 5 and six because then that allows a lot more content to be made
Here's my ideal client checklist. Number one, already on Only Fans... willingness to actually create content consistently... communication... payments... a bit coachable.
if they've gone through tons of agencies, especially in the last like 3 to 6 months... probably the common denominator is the creator.

me personally when it comes to new creators I definitely would say I'm kind of looking at the mindset that they're in before coming into this like if they're just in this I just want to make money passively then forget it it's not going to work

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no documented signing-call qualification framework (§6 THIN); this is a build-toward script Palm lacks.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a documented signing-call SOP: human-first rapport, explicit work-ethic/cadence non-negotiables, and willingness to reject misaligned creators.

new_findings.title: Match deal structure to creator tier, not one-size 50/50

variants.claim: Deal structure should flex to the creator's revenue tier instead of applying a single 50/50 split across all creators.
variants.what_they_do: Uses tiered economics: beginners via a paid coaching/productized model (not 50/50), mid-tier (10-50k/mo) on 50/50 plus an upfront setup fee, and whales (100k+/mo) on a lower rev-share plus a fixed monthly retainer (e.g. 20k/mo retainer + 50% of upside above current revenue) so the agency is profitable from month one.

variants.claim: Percentage splits with US/UK/Australian creators outperform paying monthly salaries to lower-cost-region creators because Western audiences spend more and the model carries no upfront cost.
variants.what_they_do: Moved off salaried Colombian/Argentinian models (high overhead, hard to reach US spenders, recurring raise pressure) to zero-upfront percentage deals (e.g. 50/50) with US/UK/AU creators whose audiences actually pay premium PPV prices.

variants.claim: In a vertical-scale agency, paying creators a fixed monthly salary (e.g. $2,500–$10,000) regardless of page revenue allows the agency to retain ~90% of profits.
variants.what_they_do: Agency signs creators on a flat monthly salary tied to cost-of-living (e.g. $2,500–$10,000/mo depending on location). If the page earns $100K/mo, the agency keeps ~$90K. Creator has no performance incentive; agency owns all marketing decisions. This is contrasted with rev-share models where creator keeps 70%+ and agency takes ~30%.

sources.quote: you have to have tiers... Your deal structure has to flex to the tier
I'm going to charge you 20K a month retainer... I only want 50% of what we make you above that 100K
going from zero to 10K with a salary model is like a billion times harder than with a percentage model from the west
If it's a 50/50, it's pretty much based on what you make her. She takes half
I could theoretically be paying this creator $2500 every single month, depending on where she lives, upwards to $10,000 a month. I keep 90% of the profit.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets a single per-creator commission % at onboarding with no documented tier/retainer/upfront model (§7 THIN GAP — the #1 unwritten gap).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a tiered Palm pricing model (upfront setup fee for ad-acquired creators; retainer + upside split for established creators) instead of a flat commission, to protect margins and de-risk acquisition spend.

new_findings.title: Make the fan chase to hold power

variants.claim: Letting the fan initiate and withholding rather than over-giving keeps the model in the power position and increases buying.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard plays hot-and-cold and sends non-nude teasers so the customer initiates, putting the chatter in the power position; habibi withholds (tells a fan asking for photos 'no, you're going to have to wait') to drive desire before selling.

variants.claim: Deliberately breaking rapport with playful negative statements (e.g., 'I was starting to like you until you said that') and push-pull phrasing prevents the sub from losing interest and causes them to actively seek the chatter's validation — which is the precondition for purchasing.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are drilled on two borrowed-from-pickup techniques: (1) Rapport breaks — any statement that actively discourages the deepening of connection, always softened as a joke (e.g., 'you and I would never get along because you're a math teacher and I have such trauma about math teachers'). These are not standalone — they must be followed up or contextualized so they don't read as mean over text. (2) Push-pulls — a positive statement immediately followed by a negative, or vice versa (e.g., 'you're actually kind of funny and I bet people teased you for it growing up'). These are to be peppered throughout the entire conversation, not saved for one moment. The psychological goal: sub chases chatter's validation, which increases desire to please — i.e., spend money.

sources.quote: we always want to let the customer initiate... we are the ones that are in the power position
even if a guy says hey can you send me photos of your tits you just say simply no you're going to have to wait
a break in rapport anything that actively discourages the deepening or establishment of a positive connection
you should be doing push pulls you should really be doing breaks and rapport or specifically push pulls throughout the entire interaction

palm_comparison.vs_us: Outsourced chatting; Palm owns no objection/power-dynamic playbook (§8 OUTSOURCED). Build-toward. Cross-creator consensus (Luca + habibi).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Encode push-pull and let-the-fan-initiate tactics into the in-house chatting SOP rather than leaving tone to the vendor.

new_findings.topic: Cloud phone farm for IG account amplification
new_findings.title: Operate 200–300 cloud-based IG accounts to funnel traffic to creator main pages

variants.claim: Running hundreds of warmed-up Instagram accounts via cloud phones on automated schedules generates 50–200 additional subs per day per creator while insulating the main account from bans.
variants.what_they_do: Agency spins up 200–300 Instagram accounts on cloud-hosted real phones. Automations handle: feed scrolling, liking, Reel posting, phone-number verification — all to mimic organic human behavior and avoid suspensions. None of these satellite accounts carry the OF link in bio; they funnel only to the creator's main IG page (or to 5 intermediate accounts). The OF link lives only on the main page. Suspension rate drops to ~1-in-20 vs. higher rates when the OF link is in satellite bios. If main account is banned, warmed satellite accounts are handed to the creator to restart on.

variants.claim: Multiply reach by creating multiple secondary social accounts and posting across many platforms, then funnel all of it to OnlyFans.
variants.what_they_do: Beyond the creator's main IG, create 2nd/3rd/4th IG accounts plus Reddit, Snapchat, threads, Facebook; even if a backup only adds 15k-100k views/mo it yields net-new paid subs you can attribute with a tracking link — distinct from the creator's existing audience. Post daily/multiple times, organize content a week in advance.

sources.quote: we set up hundreds of Instagram accounts... you can run everything off the cloud
In none of these accounts' bios, we're putting that link... we're just funneling to other Instagram pages
if we have 200 phones for one creator, and we're literally pulling one subscriber per day from each of the phones, then that's 200 subs a day
are you actually creating new accounts? Like... a second, third, fourth, fifth Instagram account... an extra 15, 20, 30, 100,000 views per month, it's better than nothing.
you can have a tracking link and all of a sudden you can show the client... extra 20, 50, 100 paid subscribers just from these backup accounts.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature AI-account warmup system for AI personas (§3/§4) using GrapheneOS Pixels and Publer, but this is for synthetic creator accounts, not a satellite-funnel network for real creators. Palm has no documented equivalent for real-creator traffic amplification (§4 🔲 GAP — real-creator warmup/cadence not formalized; §5 🔲 GAP — traffic strategy not documented).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Evaluate adopting a scaled cloud-phone funnel for real creators. Palm's existing hardware-isolated warmup infrastructure (GrapheneOS, SIM inventory, Publer) gives a meaningful head start — the playbook could be adapted. Key decisions: (1) cloud phone provider selection, (2) automation tooling for feed warm-up, (3) funnel topology (satellite → main IG → OF). Start small (20–30 accounts for one creator) before scaling to 200+. Flag as high-priority given current IG instability signals.

new_findings.title: Promote staff up the five levels of accountability to remove yourself as bottleneck

variants.claim: Scale past a plateau by training department heads to own problems end-to-end rather than escalating every decision to the owner.
variants.what_they_do: Five accountability levels: (1) wait to be told, (2) bring the problem, (3) bring problem + 1-2 solutions, (4) solve it and report, (5) solve it AND build an SOP so it never recurs. Most teams sit at 1-2, making the owner the bottleneck. Push C-suite heads to level 4-5; example: an acquisition manager independently bought aged warmed IG accounts and hired another VA to lift outreach capacity, then just reported it. Hold Mon-Fri morning team calls with only the department heads.

variants.claim: Founders scale out of the business by delegating outcomes and decision-making (an ownership ladder), not by dumping tasks, which only deepens dependency.
variants.what_they_do: Audits dollar-value-per-hour (only hires when a low-DVPH task blocks a high one), develops people up a level 1-5 accountability dial from doer to owner, and installs a decision filter so nobody escalates a problem without bullets + 2-3 solutions + a recommendation; uses the '30-day disappear test' to find the ceiling.

sources.quote: there are five main levels of accountability... the first level being they just simply wait for the command on what to do.
what's different between level five and level four is they not only find the problem and solve it, but they also will build infrastructure to ensure that this problem never happens again.
making sure that you guys do morning team calls Monday to Friday.
delegation actually works is if you delegate outcomes plus decision-making instead of delegating tasks
never bring me a problem without what happened... 2-3 solutions, your recommendation

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no hiring scorecards, accountability framework, or management cadence documented (§10 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt the five-level accountability ladder as a management standard, push leads to level 4-5 with SOP-building expectations, and run Mon-Fri morning leadership calls.

new_findings.title: Source creators across IG, Twitter, Reddit, TikTok, and prioritize referrals

variants.claim: Find creators by searching niche keywords across multiple platforms, but treat satisfied-client referrals as the highest-quality channel.
variants.what_they_do: Search 'only fans' / niche terms (e.g. gamer girl) on Instagram, Twitter, Reddit, TikTok and filter for link-in-bio + recent activity. TikTok surfaces up-and-comers; Reddit is where whales and creators already are. Best channel = referrals: after a great first month in the green, ask the happy creator who else does OnlyFans.

variants.claim: Creators typically use the same username on X/Twitter and Instagram, so you can search 'only fans' on X, collect usernames, then find and DM the same accounts on Instagram.
variants.what_they_do: Operator searches the word 'only fans' on X to surface creator accounts, copies their username, pastes it into Instagram search, and DMs there. They also use Instagram's 'related accounts' graph — following lists, likers, and commenters of one known OF creator — to find unlimited additional prospects.

variants.claim: Creator acquisition works by finding help-seeking creators in social comment sections, outreaching across every platform with a curiosity-driven message, and pushing for a quick call rather than closing over DMs.
variants.what_they_do: Searches TikTok for OnlyFans-related videos and mines the comment sections for girls asking for help (a signal they lack an agency), outreaches on every platform she's on with a short curiosity message ('100 fresh paying subs every single day'), and immediately pushes to a 5-minute call.

sources.quote: first one of course is Instagram... Your other option is going to be Twitter... The next one is going to be Reddit... You can also find them on Tik Tok.
by far the best is referrals... after you've delivered a great service... ask, do you know of anybody else who's also doing Only Fans.
you simply go on X and you search the word only fans on the search bar... Go on to Instagram and copy and paste the same username
within the comment section will be girls doing Only Fans asking for help
you basically just want to push for a call straight away

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already uses referrals (§6) but has no documented multi-platform sourcing process or a systematic referral ask.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document a multi-platform sourcing process and bake a structured referral ask into the post-first-month creator-success cadence.

new_findings.topic: Free teaser content as pre-sell warm-up
new_findings.title: Send 1–2 free teaser images before any PPV offer to prime the subscriber

variants.claim: Sending free 'outfit' or soft-tease images before the first paid offer raises arousal and reduces friction on the first purchase, making the entire escalation ladder more likely to convert.
variants.what_they_do: At the start of each scripted session, the chatter sends 1–2 free images — typically a suggestive but non-explicit 'outfit' shot — with no price attached, framed as casual sharing ('what do you think about this outfit?'). Only after the subscriber reacts positively does the chatter pivot to the paid ladder. This gates the sell behind a micro-engagement that confirms interest and builds investment.

variants.claim: Posting a simple 'I'm online / bored' caption on the creator's feed draws fans into DMs on their own initiative, giving the chatter frame control and avoiding the 'spammy' feel of mass PPV messages.
variants.what_they_do: Instead of mass-sending PPV blasts, the chatter (or creator post) publishes a low-key feed post ('hey I'm up, who's up?', 'come to my DMs right now') that signals availability without directly soliciting money. Fans who respond have self-selected as engaged; the chatter is now in a reactive, high-authority position to run the upsell sequence.

sources.quote: I'll maybe send you a couple pictures just for free no price attached of my outfit maybe I look cute I'm like yeah what do you think about this outfit
ideally it's something sexual that makes the subscriber horny and he's like okay yeah that's hot... now after that you can be like yo do you want me to show a bit more
post on your page say hey I'm online hey I'm bored um just post a picture caption it and they'll get the idea that you're online
you don't want to ever spam with ppv this is a huge No-No it cheapens your brand

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chat SOP exists at Palm (§8). This specific 'free teaser before first pitch' mechanic is a discrete, concrete tactic to encode into the future chatter SOP and AI chatting state machine.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a mandatory 'warm-up phase' in the chat SOP: 1 free teaser image sent before any PPV offer, followed by a reaction-check message. Only proceed to the $15 entry PPV after a positive subscriber response. This applies directly to AI chatting — build the state machine so it cannot enter 'sell mode' before completing the warm-up phase.

new_findings.title: One reusable script deployed across all subscribers = scalable chat ops

variants.claim: A single well-constructed escalation script can be reused with every subscriber, making chat operations scalable without requiring chatters to freestyle or produce new custom content daily.
variants.what_they_do: The script is treated as a static, reusable asset — not a custom conversation. Chatters are trained to deliver it as if it's personal and spontaneous (the 'experience' framing), but operationally it's the same sequence for every fan. This eliminates the content-production bottleneck of true custom content (where a new video must be filmed for each request) and lets chatters handle higher fan volume with consistent outcomes.

variants.claim: Not all chatters come with polished PPV skills; agencies that hand chatters specific scripts and strategies outperform those that leave chatters to their own methods.
variants.what_they_do: Black Cherry operates a chatting team internally but also licenses their PPV script framework to other agency operators. They provide PDF script playbooks, documented PPV funnels, and course-based training covering at least seven distinct PPV strategies. The agency owns the IP; chatters execute to spec.

sources.quote: it's easy to replicate you just have one script ready and you can sell it to every single subscriber
ideally the subscriber gets more and more horny he likes the script more and more he buys everything
you need to know these strategies so that you can train shatters to be this good not all Chatters have mastered the game they know what they know but when you can give them scripts like this
Down Below there's actually going to be a PDF attached where you can see a very precise example of how we run this

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting (§8) and has no internal script assets. This 'script as reusable product' model is directly applicable to the AI chatting roadmap project — the state machine should implement the script once and deploy it across all fans on a given creator's account.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For the AI chatting project, build the escalation script as a canonical, versioned asset per creator (not per conversation). Track which 'step' in the script each fan has reached using Fan Tracker CRM (§9) so the AI resumes at the correct point. For the in-house chat team, train chatters on script delivery as a skill (making canned feel personal) rather than improv.

new_findings.topic: Chatting audit: script-persona correlation + right-sizing chatter count
new_findings.title: Audit chatter scripts for persona alignment and cut excess chatters to unlock revenue

variants.claim: The single highest-impact revenue intervention at $50K+/mo is an audit of chatting: verify mass messages and scripts match the creator's personality sheet, and fire chatters whose pages are underutilized — this caused a same-day revenue spike.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin and Jason logged into Barry's Inflow (chatting platform) as admins and ran a three-part audit: (1) identified what was wrong with chatting mechanics; (2) verified all mass messages and automation scripts correlated with each creator's personality sheet; (3) identified and fired unnecessary chatters — reducing from 6 to 4 — freeing the remaining chatters to work larger, more active pages. Revenue spiked the same day.

variants.claim: A live audit of a creator's chats reliably surfaces significant revenue being left on the table, making chat optimization a strong acquisition wedge and a high-value service.
variants.what_they_do: Offers a free 'chat teardown' on the sales call to show exactly where money is being lost in the DMs (reply cadence, scripts, welcome messages, account setup, day-ladder), then offers to install the system; treats chatting as the core monetization service.

sources.quote: we went through all your mass messages we first identified what was wrong with your chatting and we looked at the correlation between all your personality sheets of your clients with the chatting
firing unnecessary chatters because you had like six chatters at the time when you really only needed four
you're showing them where the actual money is being left in the chats
how often you should reply... some example messages... a script... a simple day ladder

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently outsources DM monetization to an external chat team (§8). There is no documented internal SOP for auditing chatting quality, script-persona correlation, or chatter headcount optimization. This is high-value build-toward intelligence for Palm's roadmap (in-house chat team + AI chatting projects).
palm_comparison.recommendation: For Palm's in-house chat team roadmap (§8): build a chatter audit protocol as a core management tool — (1) persona sheet ↔ script correlation check, (2) mass message / automation review, (3) chatter-to-page load analysis with a defined optimal ratio. Even before building in-house, Palm should request this audit cadence from the current external team. Capture Inflow as a platform to evaluate.

new_findings.title: Master each channel yourself, then delegate to cheap geo-matched VAs

variants.claim: Operators master each posting channel personally before delegating it to low-cost VAs with strict SOPs, reserving founder time for high-ROI work.
variants.what_they_do: Run IG/TikTok yourself for ~1 month to learn it, then hand off to $1-3/hr VAs with documented SOPs and image/comment banks; keep TikTok geo-matched (US/UK/AU poster, not India/Africa) because TikTok pushes by poster location; X and Threads are easily outsourced, but the high-ROI anchor (IG/TikTok) is mastered first.

variants.claim: Learn each part of the business personally and turn it into an SOP before delegating, so hires run a documented process.
variants.what_they_do: Owner did outreach/closing/onboarding/marketing/chatting himself first, then wrote an SOP doc + Loom video for each task so new hires self-train from the document instead of hours of live training. Hires for hunger/reliability/coachability over experience (experienced people resist the system). Tracks KPIs on every function (DMs sent, replies, calls booked, clients closed, revenue per chatter).

sources.quote: easily outsource this to a VA for anywhere from a dollar to $3 an hour
master this better than anyone else can
you funnel all of that traffic over to Only Fans. And then the second thing is the chatting.
One of the first things that you need to do is connect their Only Fans account to your Inflow.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's posting is partly delegated (Amin manual posting via Telegram §5; editors §1) but staffing/hiring criteria, scorecards and SOP standards are a gap (§10 THIN).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formalize a VA hiring + SOP + geo-matching playbook for posting/social roles to fill the §10 staffing gap; applies to current real-creator posting ops.

new_findings.topic: Cold outreach via Instagram personal brand
new_findings.title: Build a professional IG persona to run cold outreach to creators

variants.claim: Setting up a credible Instagram profile with professional photos (not casual/topless) is the primary vehicle for cold-outreaching creators, and showing your face builds trust that converts to signed deals.
variants.what_they_do: Agency owner creates a personal IG account with polished location/lifestyle photos (real or Pinterest-sourced). Shows face for credibility; faceless is acceptable if IG photos are professional. DMs creators who appear stuck, plateaued, or post-bad-agency. Offers a free 1-week trial to prove value, then converts to 50/50. Moves promising leads to FaceTime to close.

variants.claim: Run creator outreach from a personal-looking profile rather than a branded agency account so messages get read.
variants.what_they_do: Send from your personal/duplicated account (lifestyle pics, normal-person vibe), never an 'OFM manager' agency account, because models skim their request folder and skip anything that reads as an agency. Stop labeling yourself 'OFM manager' anywhere; call it 'talent/influencer management' instead so you aren't lumped in with the spam.

sources.quote: you want to just make an Instagram profile, you want to set it up some nice business photos
you can offer if you find a model and you believe in yourself… I'll work for free for you for a week and if things improve then we can go to a 50/50 split
most people send their messages out from like an agency account... I would actually highly advise you to do your personal account.
please stop saying OFM manager in either your username, your bio... try to be a little bit different... talent management agency.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led outbound (§6) but no documented IG persona setup, photo standard, or structured funnel from IG profile → DM → FaceTime → sign. The free-trial-then-sign tactic is not in Palm's playbook.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document a Palm acquisition IG profile standard: photo style, bio copy, niche positioning. Add a free-trial offer as a low-friction entry for creators already earning but skeptical. Build a short outreach sequence: IG DM → value hook → FaceTime close → contract. Cheap to implement, directly addresses §6 gap.

new_findings.title: Put AI creators on Fanvue and make every asset look real

variants.claim: AI creators can't use OnlyFans, so run them on an AI-friendly platform like Fanvue and prioritize realistic presentation over obvious perfection.
variants.what_they_do: OnlyFans does not allow AI; Fanvue welcomes AI, lets you label the account AI, and takes 15% vs OF's 20%. Build the persona like Build-A-Bear (face to niche, then body), batch content for both social and the paid platform, make the IG bio/handle look like a real person (uni, city, age), then funnel reels traffic to Fanvue and monetize via subscription + tips + PPV. Same can scale to ~$3-8k/mo on one model with no revenue split.

variants.claim: Operators argue an owned synthetic creator beats managing real talent because it removes model-dependency, agency-hopping and split-revenue risk while keeping all earnings.
variants.what_they_do: Frames traditional OFM as a 'casino' (lazy models, theft, unpaid invoices, agency hoppers) where a $24k month nets only ~$6.5k after split; an AI model on Fanvue keeps 100% (minus 20% platform fee) for ~$17.5k net, fully owner-controlled and self-reliant.

sources.quote: Only Fans does not allow AI models. It takes 20% fees... Fan View... they advocate AI creators. They take 15%.
The more realistic you make it, the better your results will be... bio that maybe says which university they're in... which city that they live in.
you get 100% share obviously with this business model
It's a casino because that's basically what it is

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI personas currently funnel to OnlyFans via social (§3), distinct from the Fanvue AI-creator model; Palm's recreation/warmup pipeline (§3/§4) is well-suited to this future line (build-toward).
palm_comparison.recommendation: If Palm launches true AI creators (can't pass OF verification), plan a Fanvue home with AI labeling and realistic profiles, reusing the existing recreation + warmup pipeline; keep the real-creator→OF line separate.

new_findings.title: Train chatters with agency-owned PPV scripts rather than relying on their defaults

variants.claim: Not all chatters come with polished PPV skills; agencies that hand chatters specific scripts and strategies outperform those that leave chatters to their own methods.
variants.what_they_do: Black Cherry operates a chatting team internally but also licenses their PPV script framework to other agency operators. They provide PDF script playbooks, documented PPV funnels, and course-based training covering at least seven distinct PPV strategies. The agency owns the IP; chatters execute to spec.

variants.claim: Chatting drives 80%+ of OF revenue; chatters must have scripts with specific photos, videos, and voice notes pre-loaded so they can send PPV content in real time without hunting the vault mid-conversation.
variants.what_they_do: Agency builds a script library where each conversation branch has the relevant media asset already tagged/attached. Chatters follow the script and send the pre-matched content without interruption, maintaining flow and urgency in the conversation.

sources.quote: you need to know these strategies so that you can train shatters to be this good not all Chatters have mastered the game they know what they know but when you can give them scripts like this
Down Below there's actually going to be a PDF attached where you can see a very precise example of how we run this

80% or more should come from the chatting the messages and the tips so because that's the most important part you must have a system in place so you must have set scripts that you or if you've hired somebody to the to do the chatting they can follow now Within These scripts they should have pre-made content already there so photos and videos maybe even some voice notes

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced with no internal scripts or SOPs (§8). When Palm builds its in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap), it will need proprietary script libraries and training material. This competitor model — agency owns the playbook, chatters execute — is the architecture Palm should adopt.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Begin building Palm's internal PPV script library now, drawing from competitor frameworks like this one. Structure it as: (1) welcome/intro message template, (2) conversation bait posts, (3) upsell ladder scripts per price tier, (4) objection-handling / repackaging scripts. This becomes the training corpus for both the in-house chat team and the AI chatting project.

new_findings.topic: OF vault folder structure for chatter efficiency
new_findings.title: Organise OF vault into named script folders so chatters can locate PPV assets instantly

variants.claim: Structuring the OF content vault into named folders (e.g. 'Script 1', 'New Subscriber', 'PPV', 'SFW Feed') tied to specific chat scripts reduces chatter errors and speeds up PPV delivery during live conversations.
variants.what_they_do: Inside the OF vault the agency creates dedicated folders for: (1) each reusable sexting script (Script 1, Script 2, etc.) containing all media needed for that session; (2) a 'New Subscriber' folder with content specifically used in the new-sub welcome sequence; (3) a general 'PPV' folder; (4) optionally an 'SFW' folder for feed reposts. When a chatter replays a script with a new subscriber they navigate to the matching script folder to pull the correct assets in order.

variants.claim: Chatting drives 80%+ of OF revenue; chatters must have scripts with specific photos, videos, and voice notes pre-loaded so they can send PPV content in real time without hunting the vault mid-conversation.
variants.what_they_do: Agency builds a script library where each conversation branch has the relevant media asset already tagged/attached. Chatters follow the script and send the pre-matched content without interruption, maintaining flow and urgency in the conversation.

sources.quote: if you have big sexting scripts...you'll create folders in the vault called like script one script two Etc which is where all the content relating to that script will go
the easier you make it for them the less chance of mistakes there will be

80% or more should come from the chatting the messages and the tips so because that's the most important part you must have a system in place so you must have set scripts that you or if you've hired somebody to the to do the chatting they can follow now Within These scripts they should have pre-made content already there so photos and videos maybe even some voice notes

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's OF vault/PPV content system is THIN (§2) and chatting is currently outsourced (§8). There is no documented vault folder convention or script-to-asset mapping. This is directly relevant to Palm's roadmap item of building an in-house chat team and AI chatting system.
palm_comparison.recommendation: As build-toward for the in-house chat team: design the OF vault structure around scripts rather than raw chronology. Create a folder schema (New Subscriber, Script_01…Script_N, PPV_unlabelled, SFW_Feed) before handing off to chatters. Document which assets belong to which script in Airtable so the AI chatting system can programmatically select the right media during a conversation.

new_findings.title: Keep OF link off satellite accounts — funnel to intermediate IG pages only

variants.claim: Placing the OF link only on the main creator page (not on satellite/funnel accounts) reduces satellite account suspension rate to approximately 1-in-20.
variants.what_they_do: Satellite accounts never carry any OF or external link in their bios. Their sole job is to drive IG profile visits to the creator's main account (or a set of 5 intermediate accounts). The OF link lives exclusively at the final destination page. This separation keeps satellite accounts off Meta's adult-content radar and dramatically lowers ban rates.

variants.claim: Because AI model accounts are vulnerable to Instagram takedowns, you protect compound growth by keeping the main posting account clean and pushing the monetization link through a separate 'slave' account tagged in the bio.
variants.what_they_do: Main (mother) account posts content normally, builds followers and watch time, and tags a second 'slave' account in bio. The slave account holds the link-in-bio to Fanvue/OF. When Instagram removes the slave account (expected), the mother account survives and keeps its audience; a new slave is swapped in. Real human creators avoid this because they're ID-verified and harder to ban.

sources.quote: in none of these accounts' bios, we're putting that link... we're just funneling to other Instagram pages, so our accounts can stay way safer
we're going to take our models on a second account and then put that in bio
This is the mother account, main account, and then the slave account is where we take it at

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's current documented setup (§5) routes traffic through a single creator IG → link-in-bio → OF. The satellite layer with link-segregation is not present. Palm's AI persona accounts do use Beacons link-in-bio (§3), which may face similar flagging risk if OF is referenced. This architecture principle — isolate the OF link to as few accounts as possible — is directly applicable.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Audit current Palm creator IG setups: does every account that references OF carry the link? If so, consider a two-tier architecture where traffic-generation accounts funnel to a single 'conversion' account that holds the OF link. This reduces surface area for platform enforcement actions across the entire portfolio.

new_findings.topic: Reddit content strategy vs. Instagram content strategy
new_findings.title: Use high-volume 7/10 static images on Reddit — never repurpose IG hook-driven reels

variants.claim: Reddit rewards posting volume of decent static/short-clip content across many subreddits, not the single viral hook-driven reel optimised for Instagram; ten 7/10 images outperform two 10/10 videos on Reddit.
variants.what_they_do: Agency repurposes a bank of ~100 photos per creator to sustain a full year of Reddit posting without new shoots. Videos used on Reddit are simple (10-15 sec, body-focused, no hook/narrative) and the same clip is reposted across 100 subreddits simultaneously. No time-of-day optimisation — just maximum post frequency spread across subreddits. Goal is aggregate impressions across subreddits, not a single viral post.

variants.claim: Content quality is the single most important Reddit variable — good content can overcome mediocre subreddits, but bad content fails even in perfect subreddits.
variants.what_they_do: Start with a niche (e.g. feet), find subreddits by searching and crawling competitor model pages. Post in each; if a subreddit doesn't reach ~20+ upvotes, swap it for a new one. Simultaneously test content by finding top-performing posts within target subreddits and asking the creator to recreate them with a personal twist. Scale to 20 subreddits/account then plateau for a month before expanding further.

sources.quote: give me a hundred pictures like like have a creator give me 100 pictures and I can promote her for the rest of the year with those 100 pictures
I would much rather have 10 pieces of content that are like seven out of 10, then I would have three pieces of content, two or three pieces of content that are 10 out of 10
we basically test the subreddit… if it doesn't get like 20 plus uploads a day then we just change them out for new ones

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content system (§1) is engineered entirely around IG hook-driven reels — viral scoring, transcript analysis, inspo-driven filming. That pipeline produces the wrong content type for Reddit. Palm also has thin OF vault content (§2); Reddit uses a middle-ground asset type (SFW-ish or soft NSFW stills/clips) that neither pipeline currently covers.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a third content tier for Reddit: simple, high-quality still photos and 10-15 sec body-focused clips. These can be captured as a small add-on during existing shoots. Store in Airtable as a separate asset type ('Reddit Assets'). Do not route IG reels to Reddit — they are wrong-format and waste the hook work.

new_findings.title: Google Form onboarding captures creator profile, device specs, goals, and personal traits

variants.claim: A structured onboarding form (Google Forms) that captures age, location, device model, personal traits, and goals is the operational foundation before any work begins.
variants.what_they_do: Agency sends a Google Form at signing. Fields include: age, country, iPhone model (iPhone 13+ required for IG quality standard), personal traits, content style preferences, and creator goals. Login to OF account follows, then profile audit, promotions review, and a written growth plan.

variants.claim: A structured onboarding folder with three components — model info sheet, platform logins, and pre-made PPV content scripts — prevents miscommunication and enables the chatting team to start immediately.
variants.what_they_do: On signing, the agency shares a Google Drive folder. The model info sheet captures personal details (favorite color, food, pet names, etc.) that chatters need to maintain consistent persona in DMs. Logins for all managed platforms are collected at once. Pre-made scripts (photo/video sequences already shot) are also uploaded so chatters can sell PPV from day one. Expectations are set in writing: communication only via WhatsApp group, Monday–Friday 9–5 local time.

sources.quote: get up a Google Forms, fill it out — you want to know when, how old the model is, where the model's from, if the model's got an iPhone, what iPhone she's recording off
we always do iPhone 13 or above, they have to be, because Instagram care a lot about quality
within our Google Drive folder, we have three things. the model information sheets... the login... and then last but not least is the scripts

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has an onboarding form wizard (name, email, commission %, state, contract, voice memo — §6) but no documented device requirement, content style intake, or goal-setting fields. The iPhone 13+ quality gate is not in Palm's onboarding.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Extend Palm's onboarding wizard to include: (1) device model field with a minimum spec gate (iPhone 13+ or Android equivalent), (2) content style/persona fields, (3) creator goals (revenue target, posting comfort level, explicit limits). Add an OF profile audit step as a formal onboarding milestone before first post.

new_findings.title: Batch shoot 5 looks → batch schedule 5 days of page posts + paired PPV messages

variants.claim: Batching a multi-look shoot and scheduling all resulting page posts and PPV messages in one session prevents daily scramble and ensures consistent output.
variants.what_they_do: In a single shoot session: 5 outfits/looks, each producing ~3 photos + 2 videos. Immediately after editing, schedules each outfit's photos to post on consecutive days AND schedules the corresponding PPV video message to go out on the same day as those photos. Result: 5 days of content planned in one sitting. Uses OF's native calendar scheduler.

variants.claim: Reducing friction between idea and published content matters more than production polish, because perfection kills the frequency that actually compounds.
variants.what_they_do: Films unedited YouTube videos from bullet points with just a mic and DJI Osmo + Screenflow, uses 4-5 templated thumbnails, and delegates only short-form editing; films an idea the moment it strikes rather than deferring it.

sources.quote: say I did five different looks today I went and did my photo shoot five different looks each look I took three photos and two videos so then what I would do is I would schedule you know tomorrow these photos the next day these photos
perfection is what kills frequency. So done always beats perfect
I just like to reduce the friction between me having an idea and then making content

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social content pipeline (§1) is strong on ideation and editing but posting cadence for real creators is not codified (§1 🔲 GAP). The OF vault/PPV side (§2) has no batch-scheduling SOP. This tactic bridges both gaps with a concrete daily-output model.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a batch content session standard for Palm real creators: minimum 3 looks per session, each look producing 2–3 photos (page) + 1 video (PPV). Ops team schedules page posts and paired PPV messages together in one Publer/OF scheduling pass. Add a 'batch session' record type to the Airtable Content Ops base to track sessions, looks, scheduled dates, and PPV prices per asset.

new_findings.title: Revenue-gated hiring ladder: VAs, then chat manager, then ops

variants.claim: Hires should be added on a revenue-gated ladder — owner does everything to ~5k, VAs/chatters at 5-20k, managers at 20k+, ops director at 50-100k+.
variants.what_they_do: 0-5k owner solo; 5-20k first VA/chatter (after chatting yourself 2 weeks); 20-40k chat manager first then content/account managers; 50-100k+ ops director to execute proven systems so the owner exits day-to-day.

variants.claim: Agencies that scale successfully add team members and formalize systems incrementally as revenue grows (every $10k step), rather than brute-forcing revenue and attempting to retrofit structure later.
variants.what_they_do: TDM describes a stepwise pattern: $10k → $20k adds first team members; $20k → $30k formalizes contracts; $30k → $40k introduces KPI tracking; $40k → $50k and onward continues compounding systems. The key is that structure is built in proportion to the revenue step, not deferred.

sources.quote: you need to hire managers
10 turned into 20 but a couple team members came on board. 20 turned into 30 which meant that you started to build out proper contracts.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's org is thin with no documented hiring criteria, comp bands, or scaling plan (§10). This is a ready-made staffing/comp ladder.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Use this ladder as a template for Palm's org-scaling plan; map Palm roles (Amin, editors, chat) onto the stages and define revenue gates for next hires.

new_findings.title: New agencies should master one creator before signing multiple

variants.claim: New agencies should aim to sign two clients simultaneously: one as a hedge if the other underperforms, but no more than two to avoid operational overwhelm before systems are proven.
variants.what_they_do: Prescribes a deliberate two-client start: if one relationship fails the other provides continuity, while capping at two prevents the operator from over-extending before they have SOPs, chatters, or reliable traffic in place.

variants.claim: Beginners who try to sign many clients simultaneously disappoint everyone and burn out; mastering operations on one client first produces better outcomes.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan explicitly advises new agency operators to sign a single creator, test all marketing and chatting strategies on that one account, and only expand once they know what works. He argues that without in-house chatters (which beginners can't afford) and without proven marketing, having five-plus clients is unmanageable and leads to poor results across the board.

sources.quote: you should ideally aim to sign two clients from the get-go. If you just have one, you can stay with one, of course, but in most cases, one of them might not work out
focus on one client to begin with because a lot of beginners will try and just like scatter gun and try and get as many clients as they can thinking that that is going to solve their problems

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented guidance on optimal portfolio size at different stages of agency maturity (§6 GAP). No scaling model or client-count-to-capacity ratio is written down.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define explicit capacity tiers in Palm's onboarding/ops documentation: e.g., Tier 0 (founding) = 1-2 creators, Tier 1 = up to 5 once chatting is delegated, Tier 2 = 10+ once a full team is in place. Use this to gate new creator signings against current team bandwidth.

new_findings.topic: Agency value proposition positioning for creator signing
new_findings.title: Lead creator pitches with organic social capability, not chat or back-end ops

variants.claim: The value proposition that closes creator deals in 2026 is demonstrable front-end marketing capability — organic social reach and traffic generation — not chatting or business infrastructure.
variants.what_they_do: The consultant advises that agencies pitching creators should lead with their organic social marketing system and track record of growing audiences, since that is what creators cannot do themselves and cannot easily get elsewhere at differentiated quality.

variants.claim: Stating '50% revenue split' is not a real offer — agencies must articulate specific outcomes (more time, more money, viral growth) to differentiate, because every competitor leads with the same split.
variants.what_they_do: Agency frames its pitch around two core creator pain points: (1) inability to grow new fans and (2) lack of time to manage everything. The offer is sold as 'save your time, make more money' with concrete evidence (screenshots of revenue growth from existing clients). The 50% figure is justified by showing the creator went from $8K to $108K/month — the split is irrelevant when absolute earnings quadruple.

sources.quote: the greatest value you can possibly offer a new model is on the front end. Using organic social to create marketing campaigns and distribution networks that generate massive volumes of high quality traffic
what not to do is... my agency takes 50% blah blah. This is not a real offer. It is instead a revenue split. There is no value to that

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition system is Josh-DM-led with no systematic pitch framework documented (§6 GAP). It is unknown whether current pitches lead with Palm's organic social strength. Given Palm's mature content engine (§1), this capability should be front-and-center in every creator conversation but is not yet formalized.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a formal creator pitch deck or one-pager that leads with Palm's organic social system: data-driven inspo sourcing, AI-assisted analysis, and proven posting infrastructure. Include any growth metrics from existing creators. This directly addresses the §6 GAP in systematic acquisition and gives Josh a repeatable, differentiating pitch rather than ad-hoc DM conversations.

new_findings.topic: IG ban survivability via satellite account bench
new_findings.title: Pre-warm satellite IG accounts as instant replacement bench when main account is banned

variants.claim: Maintaining a pool of continuously warmed satellite accounts means a creator can immediately resume posting on a new account the day their main is banned, with zero cold-start penalty.
variants.what_they_do: Because satellite accounts are already being warmed daily (feed scrolling, posting, engagement), they have account age and trust score built up. When the creator's primary IG account is banned, the agency hands over one or more of these pre-warmed accounts for the creator to continue posting from immediately, avoiding the traffic gap that causes creator churn.

variants.claim: Assuming a baseline account-ban burn rate and pre-provisioning reserve accounts is required to run social acquisition at scale without interruption.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media issues 200 accounts per platform per model (about 100 active immediately, 100 in reserve), runs a daily script using public profile views to auto-detect bans, and accepts that even fully organic operations always carry some burn.

sources.quote: if the creators' main accounts do get banned, we have a lot of other accounts which we can just give them, and they can start posting on them, because we've been growing these accounts anyway
100 that become active immediately when they start then 100 for reserve... there's always the risk of having some bans

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI-persona warmup system (§3/§4) achieves exactly this for synthetic creators — a 90-day warmup produces a ready-to-use account. The same logic is not applied to real-creator backup accounts (§4 🔲 GAP). This is a direct extension of Palm's existing capability to the real-creator line.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Maintain a small bench (3–5) of pre-warmed IG accounts per real creator. Palm's existing warmup operator UI and Publer scheduling could drive this with minimal incremental tooling. Define a handoff SOP: when main account ban is confirmed, assign bench account to creator within 24 hours. This directly reduces model churn from traffic loss (a live pain point per §6 — model retention).

new_findings.title: Four-axis sub qualification (profession, location, age, hobbies) to size wallet and build rapport

variants.claim: Systematically qualifying subscribers on four data points — profession, location, age, and hobbies — lets chatters estimate spending capacity and tailor their communication style before attempting to sell.
variants.what_they_do: After the personalized opener gets a response, chatters move into a structured qualification sequence. They ask about: (1) Profession — men spend 40+ hrs/week on work and will talk about it freely; also signals income level. (2) Location — dictates tone, cultural references, and timezone. (3) Age — a 52-year-old has 30 more years of earning history than a 22-year-old; older subs get more formal language, younger subs get slang. (4) Hobbies — reveals personality and gives commonality-building material. Chatters are also taught 'level two qualification' — drilling one level deeper on each topic (e.g., 'How did you get into that job?') to extract more information and signal genuine interest.

variants.claim: Building plausible commonalities with subscribers — using a tiered system from direct 'me too' to loose tangential connections — is the single most important driver of repeat purchasing.
variants.what_they_do: After qualification, chatters build commonalities using three levels: Level 1 ('me too') — chatter claims to share the same trait directly (e.g., 'I also study psychology'). Used sparingly because it's hard to track across many subs. Level 2 ('I know someone who') — chatter links via a third party ('My little brother studies psychology'). Common but overuse feels weird. Level 3 (tangential connection) — chatter links the sub's trait to something loosely related ('I read a Harvard Business Review article about students who study under 2 hours a night…'). This is the core technique — it's harder to fake, opens new conversational threads, and doesn't require the chatter to claim personal overlap. The goal is to give a 'plausible reason' for emotional connection so that future sales requests don't feel transactional.

sources.quote: number one profession you will be talking to men okay simps Subs they are men
another one is age how old are they part of the reason of qualifying is that we want to see their potential as a customer
building commonalities this is going to be the most important section in this presentation
oh really that's interesting I was reading an article in the Harvard Business review and it said that students who study under 2 hours a night do 50% worse

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline at Palm for chatter qualification frameworks (§8, outsourced). Build-toward for in-house chat team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a qualification checklist into the in-house chatter training SOP: chatters must capture profession, location, age, and at least one hobby for every sub before escalating to selling. Store this data per-fan in the Fan Tracker CRM (§9) to enable personalized re-engagement. Age and profession data should also feed the whale-hunting logic — a 50-year-old professional is a higher-priority cultivation target than an 18-year-old student.

new_findings.title: Score every chatter on 5 KPIs to build a leaderboard before restructuring

variants.claim: Pulling 30 days of chat logs and scoring each chatter on response time, PPV buy rate, average fan spend, retention, and objection handling creates an objective leaderboard that drives all restructuring decisions.
variants.what_they_do: Pull last 30 days of chat logs for every chatter. Score each individual across: response time, PPV buy rate, average fan spend, retention rate, objection handling quality. Aggregate into a single leaderboard score per chatter. Use that ranked list as the sole basis for warnings, fires, and promotions.

variants.claim: After scoring chatters, bottom 20% should receive either an immediate termination (truly poor) or a formal 30-day written warning with a clear performance improvement expectation.
variants.what_they_do: After building the leaderboard: worst performers are fired immediately. Bottom 20% who show potential receive a written warning stating explicitly that they have 30 days to improve or they will be replaced. No ambiguity — the timeline and consequence are stated upfront.

sources.quote: pull the last 30 days of chat logs for every single chatter and score them individually
you now have a list of all your chatters with an overall score. You now have a leaderboard
You have 30 days. Get your shit together

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently outsources all chatting to an external team (§8) and has no internal chatter KPI framework or leaderboard. This audit methodology is exactly the kind of build-toward intelligence needed when Palm moves to an in-house chat team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat team, implement this 5-KPI audit from day one. Define the scoring rubric before hiring chatter #1 so the leaderboard is operational at scale. Consider requiring the external chat team to report these same KPIs now as a baseline.

new_findings.title: Standardize all scripts and objection-handling so mid-conversation shift handoffs are seamless

variants.claim: Without fully standardized scripts across shifts, handoffs mid-selling-sequence cause subscribers to disengage, and the agency loses $1–2K per day specifically at shift-change moments.
variants.what_they_do: Every script, objection-handling sequence, and selling approach is written down and standardized across all shifts. The standard is specific enough that if Chatter A stops mid-sentence, Chatter B can pick up the exact conversation thread without the subscriber noticing a change. Revenue tracking consistently shows the lowest revenue window is at shift change — this is the explicit problem they are solving.

variants.claim: Whales who experience a persona or tone shift at handoff disengage and churn — every chatter must be trained to voice the same persona regardless of which shift they are on.
variants.what_they_do: All chatters are cross-trained on one universal tone and persona voice. Whale interactions are treated as the highest-risk handoff: if Chatter A builds rapport with a whale and Chatter B takes over sounding different, the whale is lost. They explicitly state they have lost 'a few good whales' from this failure. The fix is a documented tone/persona guide that is part of chatter onboarding and ongoing training.

sources.quote: standardize the scripts, standardize the objection handling, the selling ways. So if one chatter stops texting mid-sentence, the other chatter can jump in flawlessly
it's always the lowest revenue right when the shift shifts
they all need to be cross-trained and they need to talk in the same persona. There's one universal tone on how to talk to people, especially whales

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting (§8) and has no internal script or handoff SOP. This is a critical build-toward finding: the revenue leak at shift change is quantified ($1–2K/day at scale) and the fix is documentation, not hiring.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When designing the in-house chat system, treat shift-handoff continuity as a first-class requirement. Build a conversation-state handoff field (current PPV sequence step, last offer made, fan emotional state, open objections) into whatever CRM or chat tool is used. Standardize scripts before hiring chatters — not after.

new_findings.title: Use an Intrigue-Curiosity-CTA opener and book the call fast

variants.claim: Structure the first cold DM to intrigue, spark curiosity, and end with a clear call-to-action, then move to a call instead of selling in DMs.
variants.what_they_do: ICC framework: first 4-5 visible words must intrigue (no 'hey hope you're having a great day'), then say something that piques curiosity, then a CTA (reply / send number / book a call). Don't treat DMs as a Q&A; get to the point and get them on a call. Type every message manually, personalize lightly, space sends (~20-30/hr, ~400-500/day across many accounts), and follow up daily for up to 2 weeks ('the money is in the follow-up').

variants.claim: The opening DM should be short, reference one specific problem visible on the creator's profile (e.g., wrong link-in-bio, no reels), and pique curiosity — never a long copy-paste paragraph.
variants.what_they_do: Agents identify a concrete, observable weakness on the creator's profile before messaging, then write a brief DM that names that problem implicitly or asks a curiosity-driving question. No feature-dumping or price-quoting in the first message. Outreach is sent from a personal IG account even with as few as 150 followers.

sources.quote: I stands for intrigue. C... stands for curiosity and the next C stands for call to action.
what you're trying to do is get their phone number and call them as soon as possible.
focus on the problem that specific model has. So this could be the lack of posting reels, the wrong link in bio

palm_comparison.vs_us: No documented DM script, send-rate discipline, or follow-up cadence for acquisition (§6 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt an ICC opener template, a manual-typed/personalized/spaced sending SOP to avoid bans, and a 2-week daily follow-up rule; track per-step conversion.

new_findings.topic: Meta ads as primary traffic channel
new_findings.title: Run scripted Meta video ads direct-to-OF to bypass IG algorithm dependency

variants.claim: Purpose-built Meta ad creatives linked directly to OnlyFans via the OF tracking API can deliver 50–100 paid subs per day per creator without relying on organic IG reach.
variants.what_they_do: Agency writes 5–10 bespoke video ad scripts per model (e.g. 'boyfriend films her scrolling OF messages' scenario). Creator films them. Agency runs a $40/day testing phase across all creatives for 7 days, then identifies best cost-per-acquisition winner. Winning creative gets scaled with increased budget. Links go directly to OF using the OnlyFans API tracking link, with Meta pixel attached so both subscriber volume and spend are measurable. No intermediate redirect links needed.

sources.quote: we will give creators ad scripts to make
we'll do $40 per day every single day, and we'll see which one the algorithm actually favors
we literally just use OnlyFans API. We get a tracking link from there, so we can track everything, we can add the pixel in there

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented paid-traffic system (§5 🔲 GAP — traffic/distribution strategy not documented). Palm's current funnel is organic social → OF. This is a fully orthogonal paid channel that de-risks the IG dependency Palm currently has.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a Meta ads playbook: (1) create a script bank of 5–10 ad concepts per creator, (2) run a structured 7-day/$40-per-day creative test, (3) integrate OF API tracking links + Meta pixel for CPA visibility, (4) scale winning creative. Start with one creator as a pilot. This directly addresses the IG instability risk flagged in §1 and §5.

new_findings.title: Identify and solve one operational bottleneck at a time rather than fixing everything at once

variants.claim: Agencies scale most effectively by diagnosing the single largest bottleneck — social coverage, chatter quality, chatter hours — and solving only that before moving to the next.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan walks through a sequential bottleneck-resolution framework: (1) Could more social accounts drive more revenue? Yes → outsource social. (2) Could 24/7 chatting earn more? Yes → hire chatters. (3) Could better-trained chatters earn more? Yes → build chatter training. Each step is solved fully before the next is attempted. He explicitly warns against trying to solve all problems simultaneously, which leads to overwhelm and paralysis.

variants.claim: Agencies that spread effort across many marketing channels become mediocre at all of them; depth in one channel outperforms breadth across many.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan's second top lesson: pick one traffic/marketing method and become the best at it before expanding. He acknowledges the risk of single-channel dependency (e.g., TikTok declining) but frames transferable skills as the safety net — skills learned on TikTok apply to Instagram Reels or YouTube Shorts. The discipline is still to go deep on one channel first, then adapt skills to adjacent channels when the primary shows diminishing returns.

sources.quote: look at where your bottlenecks are in your business and work from there so you know could you make more money with a client if you had more social media accounts running traffic yes okay well how do you do that
lesson two is always going to go back to marketing so many people are trying to do everything yeah just focus on one thing and become the best at it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is actively running multiple parallel initiatives (SMM hub redesign §11, AI chatting project §8, warmup improvements §4, Phase 3 Publer §5). The risk Dylan describes — solving everything at once — is live. Palm does not have a documented prioritization framework for which bottleneck to tackle first.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a formal bottleneck log in Airtable or the admin portal: a ranked list of operational constraints with a single 'active focus' flag. At each leadership sync, confirm only one bottleneck is in active resolution. This isn't about slowing down — it's about ensuring each fix is fully deployed and measured before the next one starts. Apply this now to sequence the SMM consolidation, chat buildout, and Phase 3 Publer work.

new_findings.title: Set explicit month-1/2/3 milestones with creator at onboarding

variants.claim: Managing a creator's expectations up front — covering communication channels, response SLAs, and realistic revenue ramps per month — is the single most important onboarding step.
variants.what_they_do: Agency defines and communicates: which platform to message on, guaranteed response windows, and what revenue/subscriber outcomes are realistic in months 1, 2, and 3. They crowdsource realistic benchmarks from a community of 1,000+ operators to give creators honest projections rather than over-promising.

variants.claim: Explicitly set what will happen in week 1, month 1, and month 2 so the creator doesn't form unrealistic expectations and churn.
variants.what_they_do: On the onboarding call, walk through the timeline for the first week and first two months. Rationale: 'if you don't manage people's expectations, that's when they make their own,' which are usually unrealistic and lead to early departure when results don't match.

sources.quote: managing expectations so when I say managing expectations that includes when can she message you on what platform what can she expect in the first week in the first month the second month and the third month
if you don't manage people's expectations, that's when they make their own expectations, which typically is not really reasonable... they leave.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented expectations-setting SOP or milestone framework at creator onboarding (§6, §10). There is no written-down month-by-month ramp guide. This is a gap — creator churn risk is highest in the first 90 days when results lag expectations.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a one-page 'Month 1 / Month 2 / Month 3' benchmark doc (subscriber count, revenue ranges, content volume) derived from Palm's existing creator data. Deliver it at contract signing and reference it in a kickoff call. Log agreed expectations in the Creators table so Josh can track against actuals.

new_findings.title: Price identical content by traffic source and proven spend

variants.claim: Operators charge different prices for identical content based on the fan's traffic source and proven spend, deliberately under-pricing lower-quality traffic.
variants.what_they_do: X-sourced subs (lower quality, free subs) tagged and sold PPVs at roughly half the price of IG subs; proven spenders and whales quoted higher anchors; price never discussed openly, instead gauged from chat, fan notes and tip-baiting ('show me what you think I'm worth').

sources.quote: you can target them with lower price content to increase your spend per sub
we use differential pricing to target the X subs at lower lower prices

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented pricing/commission/PPV model (§7 THIN — 'the #1 creator concern...our position isn't written down').
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a source- and spend-based PPV pricing matrix for real creators now and bake it into chatter guidance; a direct fill for the §7 pricing gap.

new_findings.title: Manufacture social proof on the paid page to lift conversion

variants.claim: Conversion on the paid platform is treated like buying any product: visible likes/reviews validate purchase, so operators manufacture social proof before driving traffic.
variants.what_they_do: Compares a profile with no likes to a one-star restaurant; boosts likes (e.g. creating throwaway Fanvue accounts via temp-mail to like wall posts) so the page looks established and trusted, alongside an optimized profile pic and multi-angle banner that show the model fully before subscribing.

sources.quote: Perform a like boost to give more social proofing
no likes equals poor conversion

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's fan intelligence/retention is partial (§9) and there is no documented profile-conversion or social-proof playbook for the paid page; OF vault/profile presentation is thin (§2).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document a paid-page conversion checklist (profile-pic/banner optimization, social-proof signals) for real creators; treat artificial-like tactics as platform-risk and exclude, keeping the legitimate optimization parts.

new_findings.title: Build a model profile doc with chatting style, persona facts, and content inventory for chatters

variants.claim: A documented model profile — covering chatting tone, physical stats, hobbies, and personality details — is essential so chatters can maintain consistent fan relationships without breaking immersion.
variants.what_they_do: For every creator they build a profile that includes: existing chatting style (especially for account transitions), height, age, bra size, feet size, astrological sign, hobbies, and other facts that come up naturally in DM conversations. Chatters reference this doc before and during conversations. Content is stored in a shared Dropbox/Google Drive the model can also upload to directly.

sources.quote: you need to understand their chatting style if especially if they're running their existing of account and you need to transition their chatting style to your Chatters
little things like their height age bra size feet size astrological sign hobbies things that just naturally get brought up during these conversations

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's external chat team operates without a documented Palm-side model profile SOP (§8 — outsourced, no internal SOP). Fan Tracker CRM (§9) collects fan data but there is no equivalent creator-side profile that briefs chatters on the model's persona and voice.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a 'Model Brief' Airtable record linked to each creator in the Creators table. Fields: chatting tone/style notes, physical stats, hobbies, boundaries, content categories available. Share with external chat team now; becomes the foundation for in-house chatter onboarding when that roadmap item ships (§8 build-toward).

new_findings.title: Commission-plus-bonus comp structure with daily ranked leaderboard drives consistent chatter output

variants.claim: A commission-only base combined with milestone bonuses, daily ranked leaderboards shared with the team, and regular chat reviews sustains chatter performance over time and is the structural reason revenue grew from ~$20k/month to $80k+.
variants.what_they_do: Daily: an assistant compiles each chatter's individual revenue and ranks them on a leaderboard shared with the whole team — publicly feeding ego and competitive instinct. Comp structure: commission only plus bonuses tied to achievable milestones (the presenter spent three days designing this mathematically; result was a ~25% revenue lift). Chat reviews: manager logs into accounts periodically, reviews a sample of conversations, records a screen-capture with verbal feedback, sends to the chatter. Every chat review correlates with a numbers increase. Pre-training filter used before formal training to screen candidates — only those who pass pre-training get to the actual training stage. Hiring criteria codified into a checklist (not all personality types are suited to the role).

sources.quote: every single day I have my assistant compile a report of the rankings of all of the salespeople because I give out bonuses
my compensation and bonus structure which I believe is key to the continued motivation of my chatters… numbers have gone up about 25% as a result

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented chatter hiring criteria, comp structure, or performance management cadence (§8 outsourced, §10 THIN). This is the operational skeleton Palm will need when building the in-house chat team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When scoping the in-house chat team build (~1yr roadmap per §8): (1) Design a commission + milestone-bonus comp structure before the first hire — do not default to hourly. (2) Implement a daily revenue leaderboard visible to the whole chat team. (3) Build a pre-training filter (written test + role-play) that candidates must pass before formal onboarding.
