(4) Schedule weekly chat reviews for each chatter, not just when numbers drop. These four structural elements can be planned now and should be in the in-house team design spec.

new_findings.topic: Agency visual map (Miro board) for bottleneck visibility
new_findings.title: Use a shared Miro board per agency with employees rated, revenue targets, and per-creator ratios

variants.claim: A single visual Miro board showing all employees with ratings, target revenue, timeline progress, current revenue, and per-creator ratios makes every bottleneck visible and creates a clear step-by-step operating orientation for the agency owner.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin builds and shares a Miro board with each student/client. The board contains: all team members with a performance rating next to each, target revenue goal, timeline to hit it, current revenue, and per-model revenue ratios. It is described as 'basic tracking' — not pulling granular IG KPIs — but the visual format makes bottlenecks immediately apparent. It is credited as the primary driver of Barry's jump from $80K to $100K/mo.

sources.quote: you built this entire like visual map of my agency that I've never seen in that way every bottleneck was just visible right there on the screen
we have every single one of your employees with a rating next to it the target revenue the progress to the timeline your current revenue your ratios for each model

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has an Airtable-based ops infrastructure (§0) and a whale-hunting/fan tracker dashboard (§9), but no documented single-view agency health board combining team ratings, revenue targets, timeline, and per-creator ratios. The SMM hub redesign (§11) is consolidating social ops but is not the same as an agency-level performance map.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a Palm Agency Dashboard (Miro or equivalent) per managed creator that consolidates: team members and performance ratings, current vs. target revenue, timeline milestones, and per-creator revenue contribution ratio. This is low-tech but high-leverage — consider this the 'operator view' layer sitting above the existing Airtable data. Could be a new view within the Next.js admin portal.

new_findings.title: Twitter/X is currently the strongest traffic platform; Reddit is high-potential but underutilized

variants.claim: Among organic traffic platforms, Twitter/X currently delivers the best measurable results for OF creator traffic, with Reddit expected to outperform once properly activated.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan's agency client reports running Twitter (short teaser clips, slightly explicit), Reddit (entering subreddits, not yet fully active), Instagram (teaser photos + Reels), and TikTok-to-recreate. Twitter is confirmed as currently performing best. Dylan validates that Reddit, once properly worked, will be the strongest traffic source. He explicitly calls out that copy-paste trending TikTok tactics from Telegram groups are 'super outdated' — TikTok is no longer the viral free-for-all it was in 2022.

sources.quote: Twitter's the best yeah Twitter okay so Twitter's the best okay um Reddit though would weigh when we start getting that going so I know that's very good for traffic
that's like I would say that's like in my opinion like a super outdated method like it's very like TikTok is completely different people don't understand

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented traffic strategy across platforms (§5 GAP). The tech spine routes content via Publer and Telegram but there's no prioritization of which platforms drive OF conversions or how to activate Reddit. This finding gives a concrete platform hierarchy: Twitter first (activate and optimize), Reddit second (build out properly), IG ongoing.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document Palm's traffic platform hierarchy explicitly. Prioritize: (1) Ensure every real creator has an active Twitter/X account with teaser clips being posted — confirm this is happening and measure click-through to OF. (2) Build a Reddit activation SOP: subreddit research, posting cadence, account warming to avoid bans (§platform-risk overlap). (3) Formally retire the copy-paste trending TikTok Telegram-group method as a primary strategy; replace with the data-driven inspo pipeline Palm already has (§1).

new_findings.title: Commission creator-filmed ad creatives from agency-written scripts, not boosted organic posts

variants.claim: Boosting existing organic posts performs poorly as paid traffic; scripted video ads designed specifically for conversion (with hard CTAs) significantly outperform.
variants.what_they_do: Agency writes 5–10 distinct video ad scripts per model designed for paid promotion — not repurposed from organic content. Scripts follow a narrative/scenario structure (e.g. boyfriend-behind-camera POV, creator implied to be on OF). Each includes a hard CTA so viewers understand she has an OF page. Creator films all scripts as a batch. Agency tests all creatives at $40/day for 7 days, identifies winner(s), requests variation batch of the winner format, and continues iterating. Distinction from organic content pipeline: purpose, format, and CTA are all different.

sources.quote: I'm talking about actual creatives made specifically for this
we'll give five to 10 scripts for video ads per model... we'll run a testing phase for these ad creatives

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a strong data-driven organic content pipeline (§1) but no equivalent system for paid ad creatives — these are a distinct content type (neither 'social top-of-funnel' nor 'OF vault/PPV' in Palm's current taxonomy; they're a third type: paid acquisition creatives). §2 notes Palm is thin on OF vault content; this is a fourth content category Palm currently has no system for.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define 'paid ad creative' as a formal content type in Palm's content taxonomy alongside organic social and OF vault. Build a script bank template (scenario types, CTA formulas). Add an 'ad creative' task type in the Airtable content ops table (`applLIT2t83plMqNx`) so creators can be assigned scripted ad filming tasks distinct from organic reel tasks. This is the content-production prerequisite for the Meta ads traffic channel.

new_findings.title: Volume, clarity, and proof drive content performance

variants.claim: Content performance is governed by three levers — volume (negates luck), clarity (avatar's own language), and proof (converts attention to belief).
variants.what_they_do: Treats every post as a lottery ticket so quantity is the path to quality; insists on speaking the avatar's literal words ('tired of chatting all day') over marketing jargon; and backs claims with documented results. 'If you're not embarrassed by how much content you produce, you're not producing enough.'

sources.quote: volume negates luck... you are not in the content business, you're in the volume business
clarity beats cleverness... speak your avatar's language

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social engine is data-driven on what to film (§1 MATURE) but cadence/frequency is not codified (§1 GAP) and messaging clarity/proof are not formalized.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a high-volume posting cadence in the SMM hub (§11) and add avatar-language and proof guidelines to the content pipeline, complementing Palm's inspo-driven ideation.

new_findings.title: Productized fulfillment makes the creator the only variable

variants.claim: Standardizing the entire delivery system so the creator is the only variable is what makes an agency scalable, reducing dependence on any single creator.
variants.what_they_do: Runs every avatar-fit creator through one standardized machine (onboarding, evergreen content verticals, welcome message, funnel, chat monetization) auto-triggered on signing; same delivery took one creator 12k to 282k/mo and another to 44k/mo with no extra custom work, at 1.6M/mo and 60% margins. Onboarding is 'complete' only after the first full week of delivery.

sources.quote: Productized fulfillment just means the same delivery model for every creator and we're allowing the creator to be the variable
one will go on to make 280k, one will go on to make 44k... and the creator is the variable

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social pipeline is productized (§1 MATURE) but OF vault/PPV and chat delivery are not standardized (§2 THIN, §8 OUTSOURCED); no single end-to-end fulfillment machine.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Extend Palm's productized social pipeline into a full standardized fulfillment system (page setup, welcome message, funnel, chat) so new creators plug into one machine and revenue isn't concentrated in a few creators.

new_findings.title: Use proven evergreen content verticals, not niches/trends

variants.claim: Standardized evergreen content verticals built on the creator's own personality outperform niche-ing or chasing trends.
variants.what_they_do: Plugs every creator into the same proven verticals (e.g. Omegle, talking-head) rather than gym/office/etc niches, posting ~6x/day on IG and ~20x/day on trial reels across 1-2 accounts each; argues 'you are your own niche' and that conversion density (welcome msg, funnel, conversion posts) matters more than raw views.

sources.quote: We have scientifically engineered specific verticals that work with every avatar fit creator
you are your own niche as a creator

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm decides what to film from viral inspo data (§1 MATURE) but does not codify reusable evergreen verticals or a per-day posting cadence (§1 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a small set of evergreen, personality-led verticals and a high daily posting cadence in the SMM hub, layered on top of Palm's existing inspo-driven ideation.

new_findings.title: Charge no upfront or hidden fees; earn only on a partnership split

variants.claim: Legitimate agencies take no money upfront and no hidden fees, charging only via the revenue split, which is itself a trust differentiator at signing.
variants.what_they_do: Takes nothing upfront and no hidden fees, contrasting against agencies that make creators 'buy their way in'; frames the relationship as partners not assets and lays all terms out from the beginning so they never change.

sources.quote: nothing up front... I've been approached by a lot of other agencies and they all wanted me to buy my way into the agency
we really strive on being partners, you know, and not just treating you like an asset

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm produces a contract PDF at onboarding but has no documented fee-structure stance (§7 GAP); this is a concrete trust-positioning rule to adopt.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify 'no upfront, no hidden fees, partner-not-asset' as explicit Palm acquisition trust positioning.

new_findings.title: Convert founder gut-reactions into SOPs so the team executes your standards without you

variants.claim: Every time the founder instinctively knows how they would handle a situation, that instinct should be written into an SOP so the team follows the same standard without requiring the founder's involvement.
variants.what_they_do: TDM's framework: 'your intuition, the way you feel, should become the rules of the business.' If the founder would handle a creator issue a certain way, that becomes a documented framework the team is trained on. The goal is to remove the founder from execution while preserving their operating philosophy.

sources.quote: if you feel that, oh, I would deal with a creator's issue like this, then that needs to become an SOP

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's systems are strong on the content pipeline (§1) but thin on creator-facing ops, chatting protocols, and acquisition handling (§6, §8). The SOP layer for creator management and issue resolution is not documented internally.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Run a structured 'intuition capture' session: Josh walks through the 10 most common creator/fan/operational situations he handles and dictates how he'd resolve each. Evan documents these as v1 SOPs in Airtable or Notion. Prioritize creator onboarding issues, escalation handling, and any workflow currently bottlenecked on Josh.

new_findings.topic: Sequencing quality before volume — avoiding premature scale
new_findings.title: Achieve a proven content formula before scaling repurposing volume

variants.claim: Reposting low-performing content at scale wastes operational overhead with negligible subscriber return and delays the discovery of a working content formula.
variants.what_they_do: They establish a minimum quality floor first — in their case, videos that reliably hit 50K+ views each — before launching any repurposing infrastructure. Until that floor is hit, all effort goes into improving content quality (hooks, formats, etc.). Only after consistent organic performance do they add repurposing accounts and VA infrastructure.

sources.quote: before you go into the volume, you have to go into the quality… optimize the content until you have a winning formula

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) is data-driven and inspo-based, which is ahead of ad-hoc agencies on quality signal. However, Palm has no documented quality-gate threshold (e.g., a minimum average view count) that must be cleared before activating a repurposing or volume-scaling layer (§1 GAP — cadence/performance benchmarks not codified).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define and document a performance gate for each creator: e.g., 'average Reel view count ≥ X over the last 10 posts before activating multi-account repurposing.' Capture this threshold in Airtable per creator and surface it in the SMM Overview dashboard (§11 in-flight).

new_findings.topic: Revenue split between subscriptions vs. DMs
new_findings.title: Benchmark: most OF revenue comes from messages, not subscriptions

variants.claim: The vast majority of a high-performing creator's OF income comes from DM/PPV revenue, not subscription fees — illustrated by a creator earning $68K from subs vs. $300K+ from messages.
variants.what_they_do: Black Cherry uses this ratio as a core KPI framing: subscriptions are the door, DM monetization is the business. They orient their entire agency operation (chatters, scripts, PPV strategy) around maximising message revenue rather than subscriber count.

sources.quote: only made 68 000 over 300 000 literally from the subscriptions the only fan subscriptions and most of the money as you can see came from the messages themselves

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's invoicing system tracks total creator revenue and commissions (§7) but no documented KPI split between subscription vs. message revenue exists. Palm's content engine (§1) is optimised for top-of-funnel subscriber acquisition; the DM monetisation side is outsourced with limited visibility (§8).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a sub-vs-message revenue split metric to Palm's invoicing dashboard and whale-hunting reports (§9). Use this ratio as a health KPI per creator: if message revenue is <50% of total, the chatting strategy is underperforming. Share this benchmark with the external chat team as an accountability target.

new_findings.title: Pin a pre-made content menu to OF page to drive passive vault resales

variants.claim: Pinning a priced menu of previously sent content to the OF page lets new subscribers self-serve old vault content without creator intervention.
variants.what_they_do: Creator builds a text-based menu listing past content items with descriptions and prices, pins it to their OF page. New subscribers browse it and request items by name; creator pulls from the vault folder and sends. No active selling required — the menu does the work.

sources.quote: I also created another menu that has previously sent out content so I've just organized it I label what it is and the price and then I've also pinned that to my page

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented equivalent for a passive vault-resell menu (§2 gap). The chatting function is outsourced (§8), so the external chat team likely has no visibility into a structured content menu either. This is a low-effort monetization layer currently missing.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Standardize a pinned content menu for each Palm creator. The editor or ops team maintains it in Airtable (listing vault item name, label, price, thumbnail). Brief the external chat team to reference this menu when fans ask about past content. When the in-house chat team is built (~1yr roadmap), make menu-referencing a core chat SOP.

new_findings.title: Send at least one PPV mass message per day — treat it like TV advertising repetition

variants.claim: Sending PPV messages at minimum once daily, using a TV-advertising repetition mindset, is the single biggest lever for DM revenue most creators underuse.
variants.what_they_do: Creator sends PPV mass messages at least once per day, sometimes multiple times. Frames it explicitly as a repetition/awareness play (Tide detergent analogy): fans who don't buy message #1 may buy message #5. Explicitly rejects the 'one message per week' approach. Pairs this with multiple free posts per day on the OF feed to maintain presence.

sources.quote: you should be sending out pay-per-view messages at least once a day if you're not you are probably missing out on a lot of money

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8) and there is no documented PPV cadence SOP. This is build-toward intelligence for both the in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap) and the AI chatting project. The daily-minimum rule is a concrete, codifiable KPI.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a PPV send-frequency KPI to the external chat team's performance brief immediately: minimum 1 mass PPV per creator per day. When building the in-house chat SOP (roadmap), hard-code this as a daily task in the chatter checklist. For the AI chatting project, model automated PPV scheduling at 1× daily with A/B price variation.

new_findings.title: Assign each creator a hobby-based niche to give the algorithm a clear target audience

variants.claim: Choosing a specific non-adult hobby niche for a creator (e.g., fishing, golf) allows social media algorithms to categorize and distribute content to an engaged audience, dramatically increasing viral reach compared to generic dance/lifestyle posts.
variants.what_they_do: Agency identifies a real hobby the creator has, then builds all social content around that niche. The algorithm can categorize the content, show it to an existing interested community, and that community's engagement triggers viral distribution. Paid shoutouts on niche pages also become cheaper and more targeted. Creator generates content organically during activities she already does.

sources.quote: if you find a niche for your model, that will make the social media's job a lot easier to find the target audience

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content engine (§1) is strong on data-driven ideation from viral inspo reels but does not document a niche-assignment step per creator. The system scrapes what is already viral broadly; it does not codify the step of choosing one thematic niche per creator to anchor the algorithm.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'niche selection' step to Palm's creator onboarding and content pipeline (§1, step 5 or pre-step). For each creator, define one or two content niches based on their genuine interests. Feed that niche as a filter into the Apify scrape / Inspo Sources targeting so viral reference reels are pulled from that niche's top performers, not just broadly popular content.

new_findings.title: Sort subscriber list by spend tier immediately upon account access

variants.claim: The first action inside a newly-accessed OF account should be sorting existing subscribers by spend to identify whales and avoid wasting chatter time on low-value fans.
variants.what_they_do: Upon first login to the creator's OF account, agency immediately sorts subscribers by total spend, identifying high-value fans to prioritize in DMs and deprioritizing or ignoring low-spend/no-spend subscribers.

sources.quote: organizing to subscribers seeing who's spent more and who's not organizing them so you don't waste your time

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Whale Hunting module and Fan Tracker CRM (§9) for ongoing fan intelligence, but there is no documented step to perform an initial subscriber spend-sort at the point of account onboarding. The whale-hunting system appears to be an ongoing operational tool rather than an onboarding trigger. Adding this as an explicit Day-1 onboarding action would seed the whale-hunting system immediately.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add 'subscriber spend sort + whale identification' as a mandatory Day-1 step in the creator onboarding checklist. Output should be a list of top-10 spenders imported directly into the Whale Hunting / Fan Tracker tables so the external chat team has prioritized targets from day one of management.

new_findings.title: Model Reddit captions on top-performing posts in target subreddit, then iterate own voice

variants.claim: Beginners should copy the structure and tone of high-performing captions in their target subreddits (not verbatim) and only develop original voice after accumulating posting experience.
variants.what_they_do: Find the most-upvoted posts in the subreddits being targeted. Extract caption patterns (length, phrasing style, call-to-action type). Write similar-but-not-identical captions. After sufficient volume, the operator develops intuition for original captions. Treat caption testing as iterative — same test-and-replace logic applied to subreddits and content.

sources.quote: we take inspiration a lot of the time from bigger models that are doing well in the subre we post in… make it similar but not the exact same

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's inspo pipeline (§1) extracts hooks and captions from viral IG reels for social content. The same intelligence layer does not exist for Reddit. No Reddit caption SOP documented (§5 🔲 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Extend the Airtable content pipeline to include a Reddit Caption Bank: a table of top-performing captions per subreddit, refreshed weekly, used as briefs for whoever manages the Reddit accounts. This reuses the same inspo-scrape logic Palm already has — just pointed at Reddit instead of Instagram.

new_findings.title: Prioritise shareable/controversial content over all other Threads optimisations

variants.claim: Shareability is the single highest-leverage variable on Threads — more important than posting time, hashtags, or any other tactic.
variants.what_they_do: TDM runs ongoing trial-and-error testing across content types and, once a shareable/controversial format is identified, they literally copy-paste the same post daily for weeks or months, observing linear compounding growth each time. They treat repeatability of a winning format as a scaling strategy.

sources.quote: we've quite frankly just copy and pasted our posting every day for weeks, months at a time and seen similar linear compounding success every time we post the same thing

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's inspo pipeline (§1) already scores reels on a 'viral score' metric, which partially captures shareability. However, the repeat-winning-format mechanic is not documented, and Palm has no equivalent Threads feedback loop.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'repeat winner' flag in the Content pipeline: when a Threads post exceeds a shareability threshold (e.g., shares > X), tag it for systematic daily re-use. Build a simple Airtable view or Publer template for repeating top Threads posts without manual re-creation.

new_findings.title: Launch with a professional booking-call website before signing first creator

variants.claim: Building a credible website with a book-a-call feature on a tool like Lovable before acquiring any creators establishes legitimacy that accelerates creator trust and signing.
variants.what_they_do: Hunter recommends using Lovable (no-code web builder) to create a professional agency site with booking functionality as step one, before any creator outreach. The site signals establishment and reduces friction in creator pitches. He also recommends scanning OFM channels for chatting agencies with proven results to identify outsourcing partners immediately.

sources.quote: I would using lovable create a website with a book a call feature and make it look established and professional.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has an internal admin portal (§0) but no documented public-facing creator acquisition website or booking funnel. Creator onboarding starts with a form wizard after a deal is agreed (§6) — no self-serve discovery or booking flow exists for inbound creator leads.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a public Palm agency site (separate from the admin portal) with: agency positioning, creator success metrics/social proof, and a Calendly or similar book-a-call flow. This supports inbound acquisition from the lead-getter referral program (see acquisition finding above) and paid ads. Low cost to implement given Palm's existing tech capacity.

new_findings.topic: Overhiring chatters as a buffer strategy
new_findings.title: Keep chatters on paid standby to avoid panic-hire after signing a burst of creators

variants.claim: Deliberately overhire chatters and keep them on standby so that a sudden influx of new creator signings never forces a desperate, low-quality hire.
variants.what_they_do: After going through a 'firing spree' from bad chatters who couldn't adapt to a new training system, the speaker now intentionally maintains excess chatter capacity — paying people to be available rather than scrambling to hire when a new account comes online. The logic: referral/lead flow is unpredictable; you might sign two or three creators in one day, and if you're under-staffed you either drop existing clients or miss the new deal entirely.

sources.quote: I actually overhire these days and like I'm literally putting Chatters on standby at this point because to make sure that okay whenever I need you I know that you're there

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced to an external team (§8). Palm has no visibility into or control over the external team's staffing buffer. As Palm builds toward in-house chatting (~1yr roadmap), this standby-overhire model is a direct build-toward blueprint. The risk of panic-hiring bad chatters (account damage, funneling fans to PayPal/CashApp) is exactly the failure mode Palm needs to design around from day one.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When Palm begins standing up its in-house chat team, bake in a standby roster from the start — hire and lightly train 1-2 extra chatters beyond current account load before they're needed. Define a 'bench' policy: bench chatters get a small retainer or first-right-of-hours when new accounts activate. This prevents the panic-hire cycle the speaker describes.

new_findings.title: Segment OF creators into 4 revenue tiers to target the right clients

variants.claim: Agencies should segment potential creator clients into four revenue bands and target the tier where their service delivers the highest ROI.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan segments OF creators into four bands: $0–10K/mo, $10–50K/mo, $100–500K/mo, and $500K+/mo. New/smaller agencies are directed to target the $0–10K band because those creators get the biggest ROI from management services. He sends clients a segmentation sheet and has them identify which band to focus on exclusively, so outreach and pitching is concentrated rather than scattered.

sources.quote: we split there up into four market segments right only fans models making 500k a month only fans models making between 100 500k a month only fans models making 10 to 50K a month and only fans models anything from 0 to 10K

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented targeting criteria for which creator tier to pursue (§6 GAP). Acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no written segmentation model. This competitor has a concrete framework that tells a new agency owner exactly which creators to approach and why.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a one-page creator segmentation model for Palm: define the four revenue tiers, articulate the ROI argument for each, and codify which tier(s) Palm currently targets and why. Use this as a qualifier in outbound DM scripts so Josh — and eventually any acquisition hire — is fishing in the right pond from day one.

new_findings.title: High-earning creators prioritize comfort over maximum revenue — manage accordingly

variants.claim: Creators earning $40K+/month will willingly sacrifice $5–10K/mo to reduce discomfort with content or workload; agencies that ignore this lose those creators.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan distinguishes two creator mindsets by earnings level. Sub-$40K creators tolerate discomfort to earn more. $40K+ creators who've been in the game a while prioritize comfort — they'll take a $10K/mo hit to avoid content they dislike or a social media workload they resent. His lesson: actively solicit creator feedback, understand the real objection (content type? hours?), and adjust the content/marketing strategy to keep them comfortable even at the cost of some revenue.

sources.quote: keeping your client comfortable with what's being done especially a lot of these higher earning clients that you'll have because they're making so much money money's no longer the focus for them it's making money while being comfortable at the same time

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no written retention or creator success strategy (§9 GAP). There is no documented framework for tiering creator expectations or proactive comfort-check cadence. This is a practical SOP gap that could cause churn of Palm's highest-value creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a creator retention SOP segmented by earnings tier. For creators above a defined revenue threshold (e.g., $20K+/mo), schedule a monthly 'comfort check' — a structured conversation covering: content they're no longer willing to make, social media hours they find excessive, and any concerns about the agency's direction. Document outcomes in Airtable `Creators` table. Proactively adjust strategy rather than waiting for a creator to signal they're leaving.

new_findings.title: Promote top-20% chatters to shift leads who own whale accounts and implement systems first

variants.claim: Top-performing chatters should be elevated to shift leads with higher-value responsibilities — handling whales, receiving best subscribers, implementing new systems, and potentially earning better pay — to incentivize excellence without necessarily moving them to pure management.
variants.what_they_do: Top 20% by leaderboard score become shift leads. Responsibilities: handle all whale interactions, assigned the highest-value subscribers, first cohort to test and implement any new system or script, eligible for increased pay tied to sustained performance. They remain chatters but are the internal standard-setters.

sources.quote: the top 20%, so the best of the best, they become shift leads. They have the responsibility to handle whales

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatter structure exists at Palm (§8). This shift-lead model is a concrete org design to adopt when building the in-house team — it creates a promotion path without pulling best chatters away from revenue-generating work.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design the in-house chat org with two tiers from the start: chatters and shift leads. Write the shift-lead job description now (whale ownership, system rollout, quality oversight) so the role is clear before the first hire. Tie shift-lead pay to whale revenue metrics, not just tenure.

new_findings.title: Track chatting ratio; target 1:9-1:10

variants.claim: Chatting performance should be measured as a ratio of message/tip revenue to subscription revenue, with elite teams targeting roughly 1:9 to 1:10.
variants.what_they_do: Calculates (messages + tips) / subscriptions; reports turning a ~1:1 ratio creator into 1:10+, and uses the ratio gap on creator calls to show lost revenue (e.g. a 1:3 vs 1:9 difference of thousands/month).

sources.quote: add up your messages and tips as well and divide it by your subscriptions

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm collects fan/whale data (§9) but has no documented chatting KPI like this. The ratio is a clean leadership metric Palm could adopt immediately even while chatting is outsourced.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Have the outsourced chat team report a weekly chatting ratio per creator now, and bake the metric into Palm's whale-hunting/Chat Team Report views.

new_findings.title: Hybrid AI makes salary creators highly profitable

variants.claim: Pairing a low-cost salary creator with AI volume yields outsized margins because output is decoupled from the creator's motivation or filming.
variants.what_they_do: Cites a student paying salary creators ~$1.8-2k/mo each, generating ~$15-20k/mo per creator via hybrid AI volume, keeping the bulk as profit since the salary model only needs to produce NSFW vault content.

sources.quote: he's paying 6K to make over $45,000

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission % per creator at onboarding but has no documented pricing/commission MODEL or salary-vs-split rationale (§7 gap). This is a concrete alternative deal structure.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Evaluate a salary-creator + AI-volume deal structure as an alternative to revenue split for select creators; document the margin math in a pricing model.

new_findings.title: Gate chatter hires on English, typing speed, and connection before training

variants.claim: Screen chatters on measurable competencies before hiring and training, since bad chatting silently destroys revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Before hiring a chatter, run an English skill test, a Wi-Fi speed test, and a words-per-minute test; only those who pass get hired and trained. Bad chatters (spelling/grammar errors, mispriced first PPVs like $4.99 or $200) are a top-7 agency-killing mistake.

sources.quote: we give them an English skill test... a Wi-Fi speed test... a words per minute test. Only once they check out on all of these, that is when you hire them.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Chatting is outsourced (§8) so Palm doesn't run this screen today; it's build-toward intelligence for the planned in-house chat team's hiring SOP.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build-toward: when standing up the in-house chat team, adopt a pre-hire screen (English / WPM / connection) and a first-PPV pricing standard.

new_findings.title: Spreadsheet/Airtable backend at scale

variants.claim: Running account and content operations off spreadsheet-style databases with front-end interfaces is the pragmatic backbone for managing accounts at scale.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media stores 10,000+ social accounts (proxy, 2FA, status, creation date) in Excel/Sheets as the database with front-end interfaces on top, updated automatically by bots, and explicitly says Airtable is just a prettier version of the same database approach.

sources.quote: the only thing better than a spreadsheet might be a slightly prettier spreadsheet in something like Air Table

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already runs on Airtable (HQ + Content Ops bases) with a Next.js admin front end (§0), so Palm is aligned with this best practice. Validation, not a gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Keep the Airtable-as-database plus admin-UI pattern; ensure account-status fields (proxy, ban status, creation date) are tracked and auto-updated as rigorously as Hive does.

consensus_risers.title: Casino psychology: keep fans in the dopamine loop and stay in control

variants.claim: DM selling is treated as gamified casino psychology where the chatter maintains the power dynamic, captures fan data for rapport, and never breaks the fantasy by talking price.
variants.what_they_do: Auto-welcome DM collects name/age/interests into fan notes for later rapport (asking about a fan's dog by name); avoid being beggy or discussing price; price-locked content keeps fans from noticing spend; keep the model 'the prize' and just out of reach; deny AI accusations and use new content plus voice notes to rebut.

variants.claim: Subscribers don't spend money on OF because they can't find free explicit content; they spend because they're buying an emotional, intimate experience that free sites can't replicate.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are trained to frame every interaction as building genuine intimacy — shyness, excitement, escalating personal connection — rather than a content transaction. The chatter script is designed so the fan feels like the experience is happening *with* the creator, not being sold a product. The script's pacing (slow → fast) mirrors real intimacy. This psychological framing is positioned as the core reason scripted chats outperform both mass PPVs and one-off custom content.

variants.claim: The girlfriend experience (parasocial connection, scripted live-feeling sells, no PPV spam) drives the highest and most durable chatting revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Optimizes the OF wall to feel like 'Snapchatting,' uses 7-10 stage scripts with verification images, never initiates sexual content (lets the fan initiate), handles broke fans with ego-protecting lines, and forbids PPV spamming across the whole team.

sources.quote: in a casino they do everything to make you blind to the fact you're there losing all your money
use fan notes to store this information
only fans isn't about sexuality it's about emotions and experiences
what he really searches for are the emotions the real sexual experience not just the pure sexuality
the GF experience, the only thing that I try to run in OFM

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm collects fan intelligence via Fan Tracker (§9 PARTIAL) but has no chatting SOP using it (§8 OUTSOURCED); the rapport-and-psychology loop is undocumented build-toward intelligence.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Specify a rapport-and-control chatting playbook (onboarding DM, fan-note schema, no-price-talk, power-dynamic rules) for the in-house/AI chat roadmap, wired into Fan Tracker.

consensus_risers.topic: Reddit asset spoofing to prevent cross-account detection
consensus_risers.title: Spoof every reused image via @spoofsybot; use RedGIFs re-upload to spoof videos

variants.claim: Reusing un-spoofed images or videos across Reddit accounts links them and triggers chain bans; spoofing tools strip the metadata/hash that Reddit uses to connect accounts.
variants.what_they_do: For images: run all reused assets through @spoofsybot on Telegram (paid, cheap, offers free trial) before posting. For videos/GIFs: upload fresh to RedGIFs each time — RedGIFs auto-generates a new file fingerprint, functionally spoofing the video. Never repost raw files across accounts.

variants.claim: Platforms now algorithmically detect AI-generated images via invisible watermarks/metadata, so operators must avoid watermarking generators and look human to survive ban waves.
variants.what_they_do: Blames a wave of Instagram bans on Nano Banana Pro images carrying Google SynthID + metadata that Meta detects and flags as fraud/deception; fix is to generate with tools that leave no SynthID/metadata, avoid OF/Fanvue logos and links in bio, list multiple socials to look human, and use lower-profile link providers (Linktree over flagged Link.me).

sources.quote: spoofsybot on Telegram… you can spoof images there and you reuse content. Make sure you always do if you want to ever reuse content, you have to always spoof it
Red GFs technically does it for you because all you have to do in red gifs is just upload the video again and use a different link
Google Nano Banana Pro has, what we discussed earlier, something called a Synth ID
the most common ban reason is fraud and deception

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature AI-persona account isolation system (§3 — hardware-isolated, separate SIMs, GrapheneOS) but no documented asset-spoofing workflow for Reddit or any platform. This is a direct gap when scaling multi-account Reddit traffic.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add asset-spoofing as a mandatory step in any Reddit posting SOP: integrate @spoofsybot into the content-prep checklist for images and enforce RedGIFs-only video uploads. Document this in the platform-risk runbook alongside existing device-isolation procedures.

consensus_risers.title: Configure an auto-sent paid intro message ($15–25) to fire on every new subscriber

variants.claim: Setting up an automated PPV intro message that triggers the moment a fan subscribes generates passive revenue from every new sub with zero chatter effort.
variants.what_they_do: Inside OF settings, a welcome message is pre-loaded with a PPV attachment priced at $15–25. It fires automatically to every new subscriber. No chatter action required. This is the first monetization touchpoint before any live chat begins, and it also flags whether the new sub is a likely spender (purchase = qualified lead for the upsell ladder).

variants.claim: An automated message sent to every new subscriber that creates a conversational scenario and pitches a first video purchase converts cold new fans into paying customers before a live chatter has to engage.
variants.what_they_do: A pre-written automated script goes to every new sub immediately after they subscribe. It creates a light interactive scenario (not specified in detail), leads the fan through a short interaction, and closes with a $34.99 video offer. Once the fan buys, they are flagged as 'warmed up' and handed to regular chat flow at full price floors.

sources.quote: another way that you can quickly gauge how interested a fan is and your content is if they purchase your intro message this is a message that you set up to automatically send out once they hit subscribe
usually our welcome messages are like 15 to 25 bucks but then the first ppv we send is like 5 to 15
it will send to the sub it will create a little scenario it will get them to do something and then it will sell them a video for $34.99

palm_comparison.vs_us: No Palm-documented equivalent (§8 outsourced, §7 GAP on PPV setup). This is a zero-marginal-effort revenue lever that the external chat team can implement immediately for every Palm creator, and should be baked into the in-house chat onboarding checklist.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Immediately brief the external chat team to verify every Palm creator has an auto-send intro PPV configured at $15–25. Make this a checklist item in creator onboarding (§6 form wizard). Track intro-PPV purchase rate per creator as a leading indicator of subscriber quality.

consensus_risers.title: Set expectations via call + concise video/docs emphasizing common beginner mistakes first

variants.claim: New chatters must be shown explicitly what 'good' looks like via a call plus a short video and documents that lead with the most common beginner errors before anything else.
variants.what_they_do: Run an onboarding call with the new chatter; share a pre-recorded video and 2–3 concise documents. Documents are structured around the most common mistakes (dry replies that kill conversation, overly aggressive/obvious PPV transitions) and use side-by-side examples — 'wrong version' vs 'right version' — for carrying conversations, captioning PPVs, transitioning, etc.

variants.claim: Before delegating DM monetization to hired chatters, the agency operator must handle messaging themselves to understand fan psychology and what drives spending.
variants.what_they_do: Requires the founder to personally reply to DMs in the early stage — not as a permanent task but as a learning phase. The stated goal is to internalize why fans message, why they send money, and what emotional triggers drive PPV conversions, before writing SOPs or hiring people to do it.

sources.quote: We put emphasis on common beginner mistakes
examples are king in your documents. Just show this is an example of a chatter doing it wrong. This is an example of a chatter doing it right
You need to understand the psychology. Why is this dude, you know, typing a message this girl online? Why is he here? Why is he sending money? You need to understand these things.

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatter onboarding materials exist at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward for in-house team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a 'chatter starter kit': a ≤10-min Loom walkthrough + a 1-page doc listing top 5 beginner mistakes with example chat screenshots. Prioritize mistake-first framing over comprehensive rules.

consensus_risers.topic: subscriber list segmentation for PPV targeting
consensus_risers.title: Segment subscriber lists by content preference and renewal status to send targeted PPV messages

variants.claim: Using OF's native list feature to segment fans by content preference (feet, striptease, etc.) and renewal status enables personalized PPV sends that feel direct and increase conversion.
variants.what_they_do: Creates named lists inside OF (beyond defaults): VIP list for high-paying subscribers ($200/mo tier who get PPV free), preference-based lists (feet fans, striptease fans, etc.). When sending a PPV mass message, selects the relevant list as the target or excludes VIP list from paid messages. Sends preference-matched messages to niche lists with personalized copy to increase perceived intimacy.

variants.claim: High-converting chat operations segment every subscriber into named relationship/spender tiers and differentiate content, pricing and chatter effort by tier.
variants.what_they_do: Four fan types (transactional, lover, whale, time-waster) marked with emoji labels in Fanvue fan-notes and placed into platform lists; mass-PPV sent per list excluding whales; same content sold at different prices by tier; whales sold to 1:1 at high anchors, never via mass message.

sources.quote: you can also create lists for certain people like if you know there are certain people that really like feet pictures or there's certain group of people that really like striptease videos you can add them to that list and then whenever you do want to send out a certain type of message you can send it to everybody
Mark all spenders on relationship types
Target each group with specific mass messages

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's fan intelligence layer (§9) has a Fan Tracker CRM and whale-hunting module but no documented PPV targeting by content preference. Chatting is outsourced (§8) so the external team may not be segmenting sends. This is build-toward for both the in-house chat team and the AI chatting project.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Brief the external chat team to begin using OF list segmentation immediately: at minimum, maintain a 'VIP/renewing' list excluded from paid PPV, and build 2–3 preference lists per creator based on DM history. Feed preference data back into the Fan Tracker CRM (§9). When building the in-house chat SOP, make list maintenance a weekly chatter task. For the AI chatting project, model preference tagging as a fan-profile attribute driving automated PPV selection.

consensus_risers.title: Set hard minimum PPV prices: $19.99 images, $39.99 videos, $300 customs

variants.claim: Selling PPV content below ~$20 for photos and ~$40 for videos trains fans to see the creator as cheap and destroys long-term monetization.
variants.what_they_do: This agency enforces hard price floors: no image PPV under $19.99, no video PPV under $39.99, no custom under $300. Low pricing is treated as a disqualifier, not a conversion tool. The logic is anchoring — cheap prices signal low value and attract fans who won't spend more, wasting chatter time for minimal return (framed as bad 'cash-for-effort' ratio).

variants.claim: Pricing PPV content by video length with a hard floor of 1 minute and a practical ceiling of ~$20 maximizes purchase rate across different fan spending levels.
variants.what_they_do: Short or clothed videos: under $8. Longer or more explicit videos: $8–$20. Hard rule: never charge for content under 1 minute — that goes free. Photos posted free to subscribers (subscription covers them); photos only charged if custom. Sends a mix of price points so lower-budget fans still convert on cheaper items while high-spenders can hit the $20–$25 ceiling.

sources.quote: you're now going to be selling pictures for no less than $19.99 each
Customs I'm not joking no less than $300

if I send out a shorter video or like a twerking video or I'm clothed then it's somewhere under like eight dollars and if it's a little bit more than that then it's somewhere over eight dollars always under I would say twenty dollars

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model or price floors (§7, explicit GAP). No internal baseline exists for what creators should charge for images, videos, or customs.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Establish Palm's standard PPV price floor as agency policy: ~$20 minimum for images, ~$40 minimum for videos, ~$300 for customs. Document this in the creator onboarding materials and share with the external chat team as a standing instruction. This is a zero-cost, immediate revenue lever.

new_evidence.title: Pre-built escalating sexting scripts are the sales unit

variants.claim: Top sellers sell DM content via pre-produced, internally-consistent escalation scripts that simulate a live session and raise price as explicitness increases.
variants.what_they_do: Each 'sexting script' is a fixed 4-step bundle (SFW starter, first-tease, jugs-only, full-nude bundle) with 2-3 variations, all shot in one consistent setting/lighting; price escalates per step; ElevenLabs voice notes add realism; script content is never reused in mass-PPV or fans realize it is not live.

variants.claim: A pre-built escalation script that guides the fan through progressively more explicit content at increasing price points generates $300–$500 per subscriber per session vs. a one-off $50 custom video.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters use a fixed script structure: (1) open with small talk / lifestyle warmup, (2) send 1–2 free teaser images to raise arousal, (3) transition to selling with a tiered video ladder — e.g., $15 (tease only) → $30 (slightly more) → $60 (light action) → $120 (more explicit) → $200 (full action). Content explicitness scales with price. Subscriber buys each step sequentially; by the final video, the session is complete and they've spent $300–$500. The same script is reused across all subscribers.

variants.claim: A pre-made DM script that sequences plain messages, half-nude images, and full-nude PPV in a deliberate ladder is the core chatting monetization engine.
variants.what_they_do: Agency writes a reusable script sequence: opener message → follow-up message → half-nude image send → bridging message → full explicit PPV offer → continuation. Recommends doing 6–7 hours of chatting personally first to learn the craft before hiring chatters. Treats this as the primary revenue driver (80–90% of OF revenue).

variants.claim: Starting PPV with a free non-revealing image, then a small $5–15 paid image, then escalating to $30–40+ video creates a progressive upsell funnel that qualifies spenders before committing to expensive content.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter sends (1) a free, non-revealing image to open the conversation; (2) a slightly more revealing image/clip priced $5–15 to qualify whether the fan will spend; (3) subsequent escalating PPV pieces priced $30–40+. Welcome/intro message is auto-sent at $15–25 on subscribe. Each step is treated as a 'drink at the bar' — the fan self-selects into a higher spend tier organically.

variants.claim: Sending a free teaser photo followed by a scripted sequence of PPV content with escalating price points ($15, $50, $100, $150, $200) converts fans into high spenders by building investment and desire incrementally.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters follow a pre-built content flow the creator has shot in advance. Conversation starts organically, then a free clothed photo is sent. First PPV is priced low ($15–20, ~15–20 second video). Free content interspersed between paid unlocks to maintain engagement. Each subsequent PPV increases in price and explicitness. A single fan session can yield $300–400. Chatters run 24/7 in 8-hour rotating shifts (3 chatters per creator account), sourced primarily from South Africa for native English and cultural fluency.

variants.claim: Monetize via a scripted sequence where each stage is more explicit and priced higher than the last, rather than flat pricing.
variants.what_they_do: A 'script' is pre-made content starting safe-for-work and escalating; price rises each stage, e.g. ~$10-15, then ~$35, ~$70, ~$140, ~$200. Between paid stages, send free photos at the level the fan already unlocked to over-deliver and stand out from other creators.

variants.claim: Selling via an escalating price ladder rather than one high ask raises total spend while keeping each step psychologically easy.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard runs a default ladder of 15, 25, 45, 65, then 100-120, and warns that hitting a warmed-up fan with a single $80 PPV makes him leave for no money, where the same total is extracted reliably in small rising steps.

palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture the escalating-bundle plus consistency rules as the core spec for Palm's future AI-chat and in-house vault production; AI voice-note generation overlaps the active AI-chatting project.

new_evidence.title: Rapport, transition, then sell

variants.claim: Every chat should build rapport and let the customer initiate the sexual turn before selling, never opening with the sale.

new_evidence.title: Automate contract → onboarding form → calendar booking via Zapier

variants.claim: The moment a client fills out a sales intake form, a fully automated chain fires: contract sent via e-signatures.io, welcome email triggered on signing, onboarding form delivered, and kickoff calendar link sent — all without manual intervention.
variants.what_they_do: Three sequential Zapier workflows: (1) Notion sales intake form submission → e-signatures.io contract creation with placeholder fields auto-populated; (2) contract signed → Gmail welcome email with onboarding form link; (3) onboarding form submitted → Gmail email with GoHighLevel calendar booking link. Appointment reminders sent at 24hr, 1hr, and 5min via GHL automation. All built in Notion with a Zapier Pro account ($29.99/mo) and e-signatures.io pay-per-use ($0.49/contract).

variants.claim: A repeatable onboarding sequence — contract → account access → OF account audit → marketing setup → expectations meeting — prevents early relationship failures.
variants.what_they_do: After contract signing (via PandaDoc or DocuSign), agency logs into creator's OF account (coordinating 2FA in real time), audits and organizes the subscriber list by spend tier, organizes the content vault, sets up missing social platforms (IG, TikTok, Twitter), sends a content schedule, and holds an expectations-setting conversation covering response windows, platform norms, and month-1/2/3 milestones.

sources.quote: e signatures.io, it's not a $30 a month type of subscription to send my contracts. It is you pay per contract sent
sales intake form to contract. So, that's what we want to do
contract signed. Send onboarding form. Perfect. I'm going to go back to my folder
first step is to of course do the onboarding right so the onboarding entails a couple of different things

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a form wizard onboarding flow for creators (§6) and auto-generated PDF contracts via Puppeteer, but no documented automated chain that sequences contract → onboarding form → kickoff booking without manual steps. palm_comparison.recommendation: Build the same three-Zap chain for Palm's creator onboarding: sales intake form submission (Airtable or a form) triggers contract PDF (already Puppeteer-built), signing triggers an onboarding packet with a kickoff Calendly/GHL link, and form completion triggers a Telegram or email alert to Josh. new_evidence.topic: Creator relationship management and milestone celebration
new_evidence.title: Assign a dedicated model-facing manager to celebrate creator milestones and prevent churn

variants.claim: Proactively recognizing creator milestones (first viral video, top 1%, highest-earning month) via a dedicated relationship manager — not the CEO — is a primary driver of creator retention and prevents the agency-creator 'butting heads' dynamic.
variants.what_they_do: The speakers describe a specific failure mode: the agency founder misses milestone moments (e.g., creator hits first six figures) because they're buried in ops. The fix is a model-facing manager whose explicit job includes celebrating wins, providing comfortability, and making the creator feel involved in the agency. Examples given: 'you just hit the top 1%,' 'your highest month ever,' 'first 1M video.' This person also handles weekly or daily content feedback, direction relays, and expectation-setting — keeping creators informed and emotionally invested in the partnership.

variants.claim: Sending a net promoter score survey to all clients on the first of every month, routing responses into a tracked database, and requiring the CSM to respond to every score — including passives and detractors — directly improves retention and provides data to improve the product.
variants.what_they_do: Notion form (NPS survey) with name, email, score (0–10), and open comment. Repeating task on the 1st of each month assigned to CSM with the form link and a pre-written email template embedded in the task. On submission: Notion automation sends internal team email alert and a thank-you email to the client. Scores roll up to the client OS (average NPS per client visible at a glance). CSM board shows unresponded scores filtered by 'responded = unchecked.' Company-wide average NPS tracked on client success dashboard. Threshold: any score below 8 triggers immediate action review.

new_evidence.topic: Fan connection before subscription as LTV driver
new_evidence.title: Build parasocial fan connection via social persona BEFORE the OF subscribe to raise LTV

variants.claim: Fans who feel connected to a creator's persona — trusting her, finding her funny, being attracted to her personality — before they subscribe spend more and stay longer, measurably raising LTV 20–30% vs. content-first pages.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin's agency builds a distinct social persona for each creator so fans are 'invested before they even spend money.' The persona is expressed across IG/TikTok (top-of-funnel), and the brand promise is consistent with what fans find on OF. This is tracked: they observe LTV and CVR lift of 20–30% after implementing persona-first strategy.

variants.claim: Agencies that maintain traffic through IG downturns retain their creators; agencies that can't keep traffic flowing lose models and revenue simultaneously.
variants.what_they_do: This agency explicitly frames multi-channel traffic infrastructure (Meta ads + phone farm) not just as a growth tool but as a creator relationship tool. When IG accounts get restricted, creators see revenue drop and blame the agency. By having non-IG traffic running continuously, the agency absorbs IG volatility without it becoming a creator-facing crisis. The result: agency kept all models during a period when competitors lost creators, and received referrals from satisfied models.

sources.quote: if you can create that connection before the only fans like they're invested already before they even spend money that's what creates retention
I've seen my LTV go up like 20 30% along with CVR along with all these other metrics

you should be providing them traffic even when things are bad

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social pipeline (§1) is strong on viral content distribution but the baseline doc does not document a persona-consistency check between social identity and OF page experience. palm_comparison.recommendation: Formalize a 'brand continuity' checkpoint: after the Creator Identity step (see content finding above), ensure the OF bio, welcome message, and pinned PPV messaging reflect the same persona defined in social. Document this in the onboarding SOP (§6). For §9 fan retention, add persona-fit as a variable when analyzing whale and churn data.

new_evidence.topic: Creator recruiting as a permanent, dedicated department

new_evidence.title: Multi-stage hiring funnel with hard filters before any offer

variants.claim: A slow, multi-stage hiring funnel with strict filtering criteria is cheaper than hiring the wrong person, whose true cost in lost time, training, and mistakes runs into tens of thousands of dollars.
variants.what_they_do: Agency runs a dedicated hiring funnel lasting roughly one week: job post → multiple filtering stages → skills/personality tests → pick only the single best-qualified applicant. If no applicant clears the bar, the owner does the job themselves for another 2 weeks and re-runs the funnel rather than settling. Once hired, the employee gets a fully thought-through onboarding, individual KPI tracking, and formal performance reviews.

variants.claim: Giving employees feedback every single day instead of once a week makes them progress seven times faster, cutting ramp time from seven months to thirty days.
variants.what_they_do: Agency owner or a designated lead delivers individual feedback to every employee daily — not a team standup, but specific corrective or reinforcing input tied to that person's KPIs and tasks. This is paired with formal performance reviews on a recurring schedule.

give your employees feedback once a week, I give them feedback every single day. That means my employees are going to progress seven times faster
